title: Rigid homotopies for sampling from algebraic varieties: a Waring structure complexity model
summary: Polynomial system solving has seen major progress in both theory and practice over the past decade. A landmark achievement was addressing Smale's 17th problem, establishing average-case polynomial-time algorithms for computing approximate solutions of polynomial systems via homotopy continuation. Recent improvements in complexity bounds for these algorithms led to the development of rigid homotopy methods. In this article, we prove a new complexity result for rigid homotopies for polynomial systems with Waring representations of prescribed length. In addition, we provide the first computational experiments for rigid homotopies using a preliminary implementation.

AI.tldr: This paper presents new complexity results and the first computational experiments for rigid homotopy methods applied to polynomial systems with Waring representations of prescribed length.
AI.motivation: To advance polynomial system solving by proving complexity bounds for rigid homotopies applied to Waring-represented systems and to provide initial experimental validation of these methods.
AI.method: The authors prove a new complexity result for rigid homotopies tailored to polynomial systems with Waring representations, and conduct the first computational experiments using a preliminary implementation of rigid homotopy methods.
AI.result: A new complexity bound for rigid homotopy methods on Waring-represented polynomial systems, supported by initial experimental results from a prototype implementation.
AI.conclusion: Rigid homotopy methods show promise for efficient polynomial system solving with Waring representations, with theoretical guarantees and preliminary computational evidence.

title: Long-time $L^2$&$H^1$-stability of the Family of DLN Methods for the Two-dimensional Incompressible Navier-Stokes Equations
summary: In this report, we study the long-time stability of the family of one-leg DLN methods for the two-dimensional incompressible Navier-Stokes equations. The family of DLN methods (with one parameter $θ$), non-linear energy stable ($G$-stable) and second-order accurate under arbitrary time grids, has been widely applied to the simulations of various fluid models with success. We derive a new version of the $G$-stability identity for the family of DLN methods under uniform time grids and mild time constraints. Then we utilize this crucial auxiliary tool and the discrete uniform Grönwall inequality lemma to prove the uniform-in-time stability of the numerical solutions. Essentially, the bounds are independent of the time interval and the initial conditions, consistent with the theories of the continuous case.

AI.tldr: This paper proves the long-time stability of DLN methods for 2D Navier-Stokes equations, showing numerical solutions remain bounded independently of time interval and initial conditions.
AI.motivation: To establish long-time (uniform-in-time) stability guarantees for DLN methods applied to incompressible Navier-Stokes equations, matching theoretical properties of the continuous equations.
AI.method: Derives a new G-stability identity for DLN methods under uniform time grids with mild constraints, then uses this identity together with a discrete uniform Grönwall inequality to prove uniform-in-time stability bounds.
AI.result: The numerical solution bounds are independent of the time interval and initial conditions, consistent with continuous case theories.
AI.conclusion: The DLN family (one-parameter θ) methods are uniformly stable over long time intervals for 2D Navier-Stokes equations, confirming their suitability for long-time simulations.

title: Geometric Milstein Scheme for Stochastic Differential Equations on SO(n) and SE(n)
summary: In the paper, we propose a higher-order geometry-preserving numerical method for stochastic differential equations (SDEs) evolving on the Lie groups SO(n) and SE(n). Most existing Lie group integrators rely on Magnus expansion of the exponential map, which makes the construction of higher-order stochastic schemes difficult. To overcome this limitation, we develop a tangent-space parameterization corrected Milstein method (TaSP-CM), extending the tangent space parameterization (TaSP) framework from Lie-group ODEs to the stochastic setting. Although TaSP is a well-established method for Lie ODEs, the extension to SDEs is non-trivial and requires new stochastic corrections that ensure both geometric consistency and higher-order accuracy. We prove that the proposed scheme achieves strong convergence of order 1 under both commutative and non-commutative noise. Numerical experiments illustrate the theoretical results and demonstrate the efficiency and robustness of the proposed method.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a higher-order geometry-preserving numerical method (TaSP-CM) for SDEs on Lie groups SO(n) and SE(n), achieving strong convergence of order 1 under both commutative and non-commutative noise.
AI.motivation: Existing Lie group integrators rely on Magnus expansion for the exponential map, making higher-order stochastic schemes difficult to construct. The authors aim to overcome this limitation by extending the tangent space parameterization (TaSP) framework from ODEs to SDEs.
AI.method: Develops a tangent-space parameterization corrected Milstein method (TaSP-CM) that extends the TaSP framework from Lie-group ODEs to the stochastic setting, incorporating new stochastic corrections for geometric consistency and higher-order accuracy.
AI.result: Proves strong convergence of order 1 under both commutative and non-commutative noise, with numerical experiments confirming theoretical results and demonstrating efficiency and robustness.
AI.conclusion: The TaSP-CM method successfully achieves higher-order geometry-preserving numerical integration for SDEs on Lie groups, overcoming limitations of Magnus expansion-based approaches.

title: Euler Singularities I: Boundary Blow-Up for Smooth Exact-Odd Axisymmetric Euler with Swirl
summary:
We construct smooth axisymmetric-with-swirl initial data in a periodic cylinder for which the three-dimensional incompressible Euler evolution develops a finite-time boundary singularity. The construction is carried out in the dynamically invariant exact-odd class \[ Γ(r,-z,t)=-Γ(r,z,t), \qquad G(r,-z,t)=-G(r,z,t), \] where \(Γ=r u^θ\) and \(G=ω^θ/r\). At the side-wall point \((r,z)=(1,0)\), exact oddness gives the pointwise identities \[ \partial_t\partial_zG(1,0,t) =
σ(t)\partial_zG(1,0,t) +2\bigl(\partial_zΓ(1,0,t)\bigr)^2, \qquad \partial_t\partial_zΓ(1,0,t) =
σ(t)\partial_zΓ(1,0,t), \] with \(σ(t)=-\partial_z u^z(1,0,t)\).
  The proof is based on a side-wall Dirichlet parametrix for the five-dimensional lifted recovery equation \(-Δ_5φ=G\). Near the wall, the effective compression kernel has leading term \[ K_0(x,y)=C_0\frac{xy}{(x^2+y^2)^2}, \qquad C_0>0, \] with controlled remainders, parity-based shear cancellation, and strain-variation bounds on narrow diagonal cones. These estimates are combined with an over-compressed dyadic angular cluster functional. The cluster functional absorbs same-scale angular fragmentation, growing dyadic windows, dynamically separated far tails, and fixed-distance exterior fields into an integrably small affine Campanato defect.
  The resulting invariant cluster contains a uniformly coherent component with amplitudes \(A_*(t)\) and \(B_*(t)\) satisfying the Dini comparison system \[ D^+A_*(t)\ge cB_*(t)^2, \qquad D^+B_*(t)\ge cA_*(t)B_*(t). \]

AI.tldr: This paper constructs smooth axisymmetric-with-swirl initial data in a periodic cylinder that leads to a finite-time boundary singularity in the 3D incompressible Euler equations, using a Dirichlet parametrix, compression kernels, and a cluster functional to prove blow-up.
AI.motivation: To prove the existence of finite-time boundary singularities for the 3D incompressible Euler equations with smooth initial data, which is a fundamental open problem in fluid mechanics.
AI.method: The proof exploits an invariant exact-odd symmetry class, a side-wall Dirichlet parametrix for a 5D lifted recovery equation, compression kernel analysis with controlled remainders, parity-based shear cancellation, and an over-compressed dyadic angular cluster functional that leads to a Dini comparison system for blow-up.
AI.result: Smooth axisymmetric-with-swirl initial data in a periodic cylinder leads to a finite-time boundary singularity for the 3D Euler evolution.
AI.conclusion: The construction confirms finite-time boundary singularity formation for the 3D Euler equations, rigorously established through the proposed analytical framework.

title: Breakeven Conditions for Beam-Target Fusion with Electron-Suppressed Targets
comment: Extended and detailed analysis corresponding to Ref. [1]
summary: This manuscript provides a detailed and extended analysis of the breakeven conditions for nuclear fusion based on beam-target interactions, distinct from conventional plasma-based approaches. Building on the energy-based criterion introduced in the accompanying Letter~\cite{TK_arXiv}, we formulate a self-consistent description of stopping power in electron-suppressed targets and derive quantitative, implementation-agnostic conditions under which fusion energy generation can exceed beam energy loss.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes breakeven conditions for nuclear fusion using beam-target interactions rather than plasma-based methods, focusing on energy-based criteria in electron-suppressed targets.
AI.motivation: To derive quantitative conditions where fusion energy output exceeds beam energy loss in beam-target fusion systems, moving beyond conventional plasma-based fusion approaches.
AI.method: The authors formulate a self-consistent description of stopping power in electron-suppressed targets and derive implementation-agnostic breakeven conditions based on energy-based criteria.
AI.result: Quantitative, implementation-agnostic conditions for breakeven fusion energy generation in beam-target interactions were derived.
AI.conclusion: Beam-target fusion with electron-suppressed targets can achieve breakeven under specific conditions distinct from conventional plasma fusion approaches.

title: GPU-Accelerated Simulations of Problems with Moving Boundaries and Fluid-Structure Interaction at Extreme Scales
summary: Computational fluid dynamics and fluid-structure interaction simulations involving moving and deforming bodies is extremely hard. In this work, we present a graphical processing unit (GPU) optimized implementation of the sharp-interface immersed boundary method. The method allows performing simulation around complex stationary as well as moving bodies on a Cartesian grid. We base our implementation on the ViCar3D framework and make use of OpenACC, CUDA, NCCL and MPI. We test the implementation across grid sizes ranging from O(10million) to O(1billion) points and achieved a 20X speedup compared to existing CPU implementation. We next present our multi-GPU implementation by utilizing CUDA streams and NCCL communicators. This enables us to obtain a >90% strong and weak scaling efficiencies. Next we demonstrate the capability of the developed software to simulate a turbulent fluid flow and coupled fluid-structure interaction in flapping bat wing in flight at Re=5000.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a GPU-optimized sharp-interface immersed boundary method implemented on the ViCar3D framework using OpenACC, CUDA, NCCL, and MPI. It achieves 20X speedup over CPUs, >90% scaling efficiency on multi-GPU setups, and demonstrates capability by simulating turbulent flow and FSI in a flapping bat wing at Re=5000.
AI.motivation: CFD and FSI simulations involving moving and deforming bodies are extremely hard, motivating the need for more efficient computational approaches leveraging GPU acceleration.
AI.method: The authors developed a GPU-optimized sharp-interface immersed boundary method based on the ViCar3D framework, employing OpenACC, CUDA, NCCL, and MPI for multi-GPU parallelization using CUDA streams and NCCL communicators.
AI.result: Achieved 20X speedup over CPU implementation across grid sizes from O(10M) to O(1B) points. Multi-GPU implementation achieved >90% strong and weak scaling efficiencies.
AI.conclusion: The developed GPU-optimized software successfully enables high-performance simulation of complex turbulent flow and FSI problems, demonstrated on a flapping bat wing in flight at Re=5000.

title: CDFCI: High-Performance Parallel Software for Many-Body Large-Scale Eigenvalue Problems
summary: CDFCI is a shared-memory parallel numerical program for computing low-lying eigenpairs of large-scale, non-relativistic fermionic Hamiltonians. The software is designed to handle a broad class of many-body quantum models, including both ab initio electronic structure Hamiltonians and lattice-based Hamiltonians arising in condensed matter physics. CDFCI combines an efficient coordinate-descent-based selected configuration interaction algorithm with dedicated parallelization strategies, achieving high performance on modern multi-core architectures. Benchmark results on representative quantum chemistry and condensed matter test cases demonstrate that CDFCI attains state-of-the-art accuracy with competitive performance compared to established selected configuration interaction (such as CIPSI or SHCI) and DMRG implementations. The software is open-source, extensively documented, and provides a Python interface for seamless integration with PySCF and other many-body simulation workflows.

AI.tldr: CDFCI is a high-performance, open-source parallel software for computing eigenpairs of large-scale fermionic Hamiltonians using a coordinate-descent-based selected configuration interaction algorithm, with competitive accuracy and efficiency against methods like CIPSI, SHCI, and DMRG.
AI.motivation: To develop an efficient, parallelized selected configuration interaction method capable of handling both ab initio electronic structure and condensed matter lattice Hamiltonians with state-of-the-art accuracy on modern multi-core architectures.
AI.method: Combines a coordinate-descent-based selected configuration interaction algorithm with dedicated shared-memory parallelization strategies, and provides a Python interface for integration with PySCF and other workflows.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art accuracy with competitive performance compared to established methods (CIPSI, SHCI, DMRG) on representative quantum chemistry and condensed matter benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: CDFCI is an open-source, extensively documented, high-performance tool that effectively addresses large-scale fermionic eigenpair problems across multiple domains.

title: Neural-Guided Domain Restriction to Accelerate Pseudospectra Computation for Structured Non-normal Banded Matrices
summary:
Computing pseudospectra of non-normal matrices is essential for understanding the stability and transient behavior of dynamical systems. Such analysis is critical in applications including fluid dynamics, control systems, and differential operators, where non-normality can lead to significant transient amplification and sensitivity to perturbations that are not captured by eigenvalue analysis alone. At large scales, commonly used numerical approaches for pseudospectra computation can become computationally demanding, as they require repeated auxiliary computations to identify spectrally sensitive regions in the complex plane.
  We present a neural network-based approach that predicts sensitive regions directly from matrix features, thereby avoiding exhaustive pseudospectra evaluation across the entire complex plane. We calibrate the prediction threshold on validation data to ensure reliable coverage of sensitive regions. The trained neural network guides the selection of grid points requiring full computation, enabling focused computation only where necessary. The approach provides a practical preprocessing strategy for efficient pseudospectra computation. Numerical experiments on non-normal banded matrices demonstrate substantial speedup compared to full grid-based numerical evaluation while maintaining high accuracy in identifying sensitive regions.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a neural network-based approach to predict spectrally sensitive regions of non-normal matrices, enabling efficient pseudospectra computation by focusing only on necessary grid points, achieving substantial speedup while maintaining accuracy on banded matrices.
AI.motivation: Computing pseudospectra of non-normal matrices is computationally demanding at large scales due to repeated auxiliary computations across the complex plane. The goal is to reduce this computational burden.
AI.method: A neural network predicts sensitive regions directly from matrix features. The prediction threshold is calibrated on validation data for reliable coverage. The network guides selection of grid points requiring full computation, enabling focused computation only where necessary.
AI.result: Numerical experiments on non-normal banded matrices demonstrate substantial speedup compared to full grid-based numerical evaluation while maintaining high accuracy in identifying sensitive regions.
AI.conclusion: The approach provides a practical preprocessing strategy for efficient pseudospectra computation, avoiding exhaustive evaluation across the entire complex plane.

title: Blow-Up Criteria and Weak--Strong Uniqueness for Compressible Fluid--Viscoelastic Shell Interactions
summary: Existence and uniqueness of strong solutions to a barotropic compressible fluid--viscoelastic shell interaction system have recently been established on a finite time interval. A natural question is whether such solutions can be continued globally. In this work, we derive a continuation criterion for this coupled system. Our analysis is based on an energy estimate at the level of material acceleration, derived under Serrin-type and Beale--Kato--Majda-type control assumptions. While in the incompressible setting, such control is sufficient to prevent finite-time blow-up, in the compressible regime it does not by itself ensure propagation of the full regularity required for strong solutions. To obtain a genuine continuation criterion, we impose a Beale--Kato--Majda Lipschitz-type control on the density and velocity gradients with stronger time integrability. In combination with the control framework underlying the acceleration estimate, we close a higher-order energy estimate and thereby prevent loss of strong-solution regularity. Consequently, the solution can be extended beyond a potential blow-up time, provided that the corresponding control norms remain finite. We further establish a weak-strong uniqueness principle for the system under the above conditional regularity criterion.

AI.tldr: This paper derives a continuation criterion for strong solutions to a barotropic compressible fluid-viscoelastic shell interaction system, showing that under Beale-Kato-Majda-type Lipschitz control on density and velocity gradients with stronger time integrability, solutions can be extended globally beyond potential blow-up times. A weak-strong uniqueness principle is also established.
AI.motivation: While existence and uniqueness of strong solutions for this coupled system have been established on finite time intervals, it was unknown whether solutions could be extended globally. The paper aims to derive a continuation criterion to determine when solutions can be continued beyond potential blow-up times.
AI.method: The analysis is based on an energy estimate at the level of material acceleration under Serrin-type and Beale-Kato-Majda-type control assumptions. To obtain a genuine continuation criterion, the authors impose a Beale-Kato-Majda Lipschitz-type control on density and velocity gradients with stronger time integrability, which is combined with the acceleration estimate framework to close a higher-order energy estimate and prevent loss of strong-solution regularity.
AI.result: The solution can be extended beyond a potential blow-up time provided that the corresponding control norms remain finite. Additionally, a weak-strong uniqueness principle is established under the conditional regularity criterion.
AI.conclusion: The Beale-Kato-Majda Lipschitz-type control with stronger time integrability is sufficient to prevent finite-time blow-up in the compressible regime (unlike the incompressible case where simpler control suffices), enabling global continuation of strong solutions and establishing weak-strong uniqueness.

title: Transition from Zonal Flows to Streamer like structures and associated edge Fluctuations
summary: We report experimental observations of a controlled transition from a zonal-flow (ZF) dominated regime to a coexistence regime of ZFs and streamers, and finally to a streamer-dominated state in a linear magnetized plasma column. The controlling parameter is the ion-neutral collision frequency. At low collisionality (2 x 10^-5 mbar), the plasma turbulence is dominated by coherent ZFs (600-700 Hz) that are nonlinearly driven by drift-wave fluctuations. With increasing collisionality (5 x 10^-4 mbar), the ZF growth is reduced and streamers emerge through nonlinear coupling of neighboring drift modes mediated by a mediator mode. At high collisionality (2 x 10^-3 mbar), ZFs are strongly damped and the turbulence becomes streamer-dominated. For each of these turbulent states, the corresponding edge fluctuations transition from coherent, symmetric to intermittent, asymmetric fluctuations with enhanced low-frequency content and larger spatial scales that can result in convective transport. Our results demonstrate the possibility of selective excitation of ZFs and streamers by regulating their collisional damping and establish the ion-neutral collision frequency as an effective control knob for regulating turbulent structures and edge transport in magnetized plasmas.

AI.tldr: This paper experimentally demonstrates that by controlling ion-neutral collision frequency, plasma turbulence can be transitioned from zonal-flow-dominated to streamer-dominated regimes in a linear magnetized plasma column, with corresponding changes in edge fluctuation characteristics and transport.
AI.motivation: To understand how to selectively excite zonal flows and streamers by regulating collisional damping, establishing collision frequency as an effective control knob for turbulent structures and edge transport in magnetized plasmas.
AI.method: Experiments in a linear magnetized plasma column controlling ion-neutral collision frequency across three regimes: low (2×10⁻⁵ mbar), intermediate (5×10⁻⁴ mbar), and high (2×10⁻³ mbar), observing transitions in turbulence structures (zonal flows vs. streamers) and edge fluctuation characteristics.
AI.result: At low collisionality: coherent ZFs (600-700 Hz) dominate. At intermediate: ZF growth reduced, streamers emerge via nonlinear coupling. At high: ZFs strongly damped, streamers dominate. Edge fluctuations transition from coherent/symmetric to intermittent/asymmetric with enhanced low-frequency content and larger spatial scales.
AI.conclusion: Ion-neutral collision frequency serves as an effective control knob for regulating turbulent structures (ZFs vs. streamers) and edge transport in magnetized plasmas.

title: An MRI-informed poromechanical model for organ-scale prediction of glioma growth
summary: Gliomas constitute one of the most aggressive and heterogeneous forms of brain tumors, posing major challenges for understanding their biology and developing effective treatments. Animal models enable the collection of rich longitudinal datasets describing tumor dynamics, which can be integrated within mathematical models to elucidate the biological mechanisms governing tumor growth. While most formulations rely on reaction-diffusion systems with limited insight on tissue deformation and fluid transport, we propose a magnetic resonance imaging (MRI)-informed, poroelastic model to describe C6 glioma growth in rats. We use data from animals (n=4) that were imaged five times after intracranial injection of cancer cells. Each MRI dataset includes (i) anatomical T1-weighted data for brain and tumor segmentation and to assign mechanical properties; (ii) diffusion-weighted MRI, which enables estimation of the fraction of each voxel that is tumor; and (iii) dynamic contrast-enhanced MRI, which informs permeability as well as vascular and liquid fraction maps. Using finite-element simulations, model calibration for each rat uses the Levenberg-Marquardt method informed by the first three MRI datasets. Tumor forecasts are validated by assessing model-data agreement on the remaining two MRI datasets. Our results show relative tumor volume errors between 0.94 percent and 11.27 percent at calibration, and prediction errors between 4.73 percent and 36.03 percent. Additionally, Dice scores ranged from 0.80 to 0.93 during calibration, and from 0.75 to 0.93 during validation. Thus, our results suggest that our poromechanical model can describe C6 glioma growth. This study provides a first step toward a patient-specific, multiscale model of the spatiotemporal poromechanics underlying glioma progression and therapeutic response.

AI.tldr: This paper develops an MRI-informed poroelastic model to describe C6 glioma growth in rats, using finite-element simulations calibrated with the Levenberg-Marquardt method. The model achieved low volume errors (0.94-11.27% at calibration, 4.73-36.03% in prediction) and high Dice scores (0.80-0.93 calibration, 0.75-0.93 validation), suggesting poromechanical models can effectively describe glioma growth dynamics.
AI.motivation: Gliomas are aggressive, heterogeneous brain tumors that are difficult to understand and treat. Existing mathematical models rely on reaction-diffusion systems that lack insight into tissue deformation and fluid transport, motivating the need for more comprehensive poroelastic models informed by rich MRI data.
AI.method: A poroelastic model informed by multimodal MRI data (T1-weighted, diffusion-weighted, and dynamic contrast-enhanced MRI) was developed. Finite-element simulations were used, and model calibration for each rat employed the Levenberg-Marquardt method using the first three of five MRI datasets. Tumor forecasts were validated against the remaining two datasets.
AI.result: Relative tumor volume errors: 0.94%-11.27% at calibration, 4.73%-36.03% during prediction. Dice scores: 0.80-0.93 at calibration, 0.75-0.93 during validation.
AI.conclusion: The poromechanical model can effectively describe C6 glioma growth in rats, providing a foundation toward patient-specific, multiscale models of glioma spatiotemporal poromechanics and therapeutic response.

title: Mixed Finite Elements for Geometrically Exact Beams using Discontinuous Rotations and Discrete Curvature
summary: We propose a novel mixed finite-element formulation for geometrically exact (Simo--Reissner) beams that introduces the moment vector as additional independent field. The specific mixed form allows for an element-local, discontinuous approximation of rotations, which is key to a simple and efficient discretization framework. The concept of discrete curvature provides a mathematically consistent treatment of rotation discontinuities. For linear constitutive laws, the mixed form is derived via a Legendre transform of the curvature-related strain energy. Objectivity is retained at the discrete level by interpolating relative rotations through a multiplicative split of the rotation field; path-independence is inherent to the total Lagrangian setting and verified numerically. Several benchmarks demonstrate optimal rates of convergence and accuracy, irrespective of the beam's slenderness and order of approximation. Notably, the lowest-order element entirely avoids rotation interpolation by employing element-constant rotations only.

AI.tldr: A mixed finite-element formulation for geometrically exact beams that introduces moment as an independent field, enabling element-local discontinuous rotation approximations with optimal convergence regardless of beam slenderness.
AI.motivation: To develop a simple and efficient discretization framework for geometrically exact (Simo-Reissner) beams by avoiding complex rotation interpolation, particularly enabling a lowest-order element with only element-constant rotations.
AI.method: Mixed finite-element formulation using Legendre transform of curvature-related strain energy; multiplicative split of rotation field for discrete objectivity; discontinuous rotation approximation with discrete curvature concept for consistent treatment of discontinuities.
AI.result: Optimal rates of convergence and accuracy demonstrated across benchmarks, irrespective of beam slenderness and approximation order; lowest-order element works with element-constant rotations only, entirely avoiding rotation interpolation.
AI.conclusion: The proposed mixed formulation successfully enables simple, efficient, and objectively discretized geometrically exact beams with path-independence verified numerically.

title: Global spherically symmetric solutions to the multidimensional isentropic compressible Navier--Stokes--Korteweg system with large initial data
summary: In this paper, we investigate the global existence of spherically symmetric strong solutions with large initial data to an initial-boundary value problem of the multidimensional isentropic compressible Navier-Stokes-Korteweg system in an unbounded exterior domain. We consider the case when the pressure $p(ρ)=ρ^γ$, the viscosity coefficients $μ(ρ)$ and $ λ(ρ)$ satisfy either $μ(ρ)=\tildeμ, λ(ρ)=\tildeλρ^α$ or $μ(ρ)=\tildeμρ^α, λ(ρ)=\tildeλρ^α$, and the capillarity coefficient $κ(ρ)=\tildeκρ^β$, where $α,β,γ\in \mathbb{R}$ are parameters, and $\tildeμ,\tildeλ,\tildeκ$ are given real constants. Under suitable restrictions on the parameters $α,β$ and $γ$, we establish the global existence and uniqueness of spherically symmetric strong solutions. The proof relies on the radically weighted energy method combined with the technique developed by Y.~Kanel'\cite{28}.

AI.tldr: This paper proves the global existence and uniqueness of spherically symmetric strong solutions for the compressible Navier-Stokes-Korteweg system with large initial data in an unbounded exterior domain, using a radially weighted energy method combined with Kanel' technique, under specific parameter restrictions on viscosity and capillarity coefficients.
AI.motivation: To investigate the global existence of spherically symmetric strong solutions with large initial data for the multidimensional isentropic compressible Navier-Stokes-Korteweg system in an unbounded exterior domain, which is a mathematically challenging problem due to the combination of nonlinearities, large data, and exterior domain geometry.
AI.method: The proof relies on a radically weighted energy method combined with the technique developed by Y. Kanel' (reference [28]). The analysis considers specific forms of viscosity coefficients μ(ρ) and λ(ρ), and capillarity coefficient κ(ρ) with parameters α, β, γ, and imposes suitable restrictions to establish the results.
AI.result: Under suitable restrictions on the parameters α, β, and γ, the global existence and uniqueness of spherically symmetric strong solutions are established for large initial data.
AI.conclusion: The paper successfully extends the global existence theory for compressible Navier-Stokes-Korteweg systems to spherically symmetric strong solutions with large initial data in unbounded exterior domains, providing a theoretical foundation for further studies in related models.

title: Synthetic model of gamma-ray emission during DT experiments on the SPARC tokamak
summary: In thermonuclear plasmas, plasma ions undergoing nuclear reactions emit gamma-rays with energies in the MeV range. Their spectroscopy can convey much plasma information, such as the DT fusion power, the spatial and velocity distributions of the fast ions, and the plasma heating performance. In the present work, we simulate the gamma-ray emission expected in the SPARC tokamak during a primary reference discharge, when the tokamak is expected to generate $140$ MW of fusion power and reach an energy gain factor of $Q\approx11$. We focus particularly T(D, $γ$)He-5, B-10(He-4, p $γ$)C-13 and D(He-3, $γ$)Li-5 reactions. We use realistic plasma profiles calculated with the TRANSP code and simulate radiofrequency heating of the plasma with CQL3D and TORIC. Possible locations for gamma spectrometers based on lanthanum bromide inorganic scintillators are suggested. For each, the signal-to-noise ratio of gamma-rays over neutrons is evaluated using the ray-tracing code ToFu and high fidelity Monte Carlo models (MCNP and OpenMC) to solve radiation transport in SPARC. A dedicated neutron attenuator made of high density polyethylene is scoped to allow gamma-spectroscopy during high neutron yield experiments. And finally, the performance of LaBr$_3$ detectors in reconstructing the fusion power generated by SPARC is discussed.

AI.tldr: This paper simulates gamma-ray emission in the SPARC tokamak using TRANSP, CQL3D, TORIC, MCNP, and OpenMC codes, focusing on T(D,γ)He-5, B-10(He-4,pγ)C-13, and D(He-3,γ)Li-5 reactions. It evaluates LaBr₃ scintillator locations, signal-to-noise ratios, neutron attenuator design, and fusion power reconstruction capability for a 140 MW, Q≈11 discharge.
AI.motivation: Gamma-ray spectroscopy in the MeV range can provide critical plasma information such as DT fusion power, fast ion spatial/velocity distributions, and plasma heating performance, but requires evaluation for high-performance tokamaks like SPARC.
AI.method: Realistic plasma profiles from TRANSP, RF heating simulation with CQL3D and TORIC, radiation transport via MCNP and OpenMC, and ray-tracing with ToFu to evaluate gamma-ray detection at multiple LaBr₃ scintillator locations.
AI.result: Possible LaBr₃ detector locations identified, signal-to-noise ratios evaluated, and a high-density polyethylene neutron attenuator designed to enable gamma-spectroscopy during high neutron yield experiments.
AI.conclusion: LaBr₃ detectors show promise for reconstructing fusion power in SPARC, with the neutron attenuator being critical for viable gamma-spectroscopy during high-yield operation.

title: Heat and mass transfer through fabric: a model for fabric drying with heated cylinders
summary: Textile drying is a key operation in the textile production cycle as it represents one of the most energy-intensive stages and plays a critical role in determining both product quality and overall process efficiency. In this work we propose a mathematical model for the drying process of a generic textile material using heated cylinders, operating under low-pressure conditions. The model's parameters are estimated by nonlinear least squares regression. Given a specific fabric, the developed model allows to predict the drying time and the residual moisture content. The model is validated using real world data provided by a major Italian textile company.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a mathematical model for textile drying using heated cylinders under low-pressure conditions. Model parameters are estimated via nonlinear least squares regression, and the model predicts drying time and residual moisture content. It is validated using real data from a major Italian textile company.
AI.motivation: Textile drying is a critical and energy-intensive stage in textile production. The goal is to develop a predictive model to improve process efficiency and product quality.
AI.method: A mathematical model is proposed for the drying process using heated cylinders under low pressure. Parameters are estimated via nonlinear least squares regression.
AI.result: The model predicts drying time and residual moisture content for a given fabric. It is validated with real-world data from an Italian textile company.
AI.conclusion: The model effectively predicts key drying parameters, contributing to improved energy efficiency and process control in textile manufacturing.

title: Existence, uniqueness, stability, and monotonicity of traveling waves for repulsion/attraction chemotaxis models with logistic type source
summary: This paper is devoted to the study of existence, uniqueness, stability, and monotonicity of traveling wave solutions to the following parabolic-elliptic chemotaxis system with logistic type source \begin{equation}\label{E:main-abstract-eq}\tag{CM} \begin{cases} u_t=u_{xx}-χ(u^m v_x)_x +u(1-u^α),\quad &x\in\mathbb{R}\cr 0=v_{xx}-v+u^γ,\quad&x\in\mathbb{R}, \end{cases} \end{equation} where $m,α,γ\ge 1$ and $χ\in\mathbb{R}$. System (CM) can be used to describe the evolution of a biological species influenced by a chemical substance produced by the species itself. In this context, the function $u$ denotes the population density of the biological species, and $v$ denotes the concentration of the chemical agent. Traveling wave solutions of (CM) connecting the two constant solutions $(1,1)$ and $(0,0)$ are among important types of solutions, which characterize the front propagation phenomena in (CM). The existence of such traveling wave solutions to (CM) with $m=α=γ=1$ has been studied in several papers. However, there is little study on the uniqueness, stability, and monotonicity of traveling wave solutions of (CM) in literature and there is also no study on the existence of traveling wave solutions of (CM) connecting $(1,1)$ and $(0,0)$ for general $m,α,γ\ge 1$. In the current paper, we prove the existence of traveling wave solutions of (CM) connecting $(1,1)$ and $(0,0)$ for any $χ\le 0$ with speed $c$ large than some number $c^*_{χ,m,γ}$, or for $0<χ<1/2$ with any speed $c>2$. We prove that the traveling wave solutions are monotone when $χ\le 0$. We also prove the uniqueness and stability of traveling wave solutions of (CM) connecting $(1,1)$ and $(0,0)$ when the speed $c$ is larger than some number $c^{**}_{χ,m,α, γ}(\ge c^*_{χ,m,γ})$.

AI.tldr: This paper proves existence, uniqueness, stability, and monotonicity of traveling wave solutions for a parabolic-elliptic chemotaxis system with logistic source, for general parameters m, α, γ ≥ 1, under certain conditions on χ and wave speed c.
AI.motivation: Prior work focused on the special case m=α=γ=1, leaving gaps in understanding for general parameters and no studies on uniqueness, stability, or monotonicity of traveling waves for this system.
AI.method: The authors employ rigorous mathematical analysis to establish existence (via conditions on χ ≤ 0 or 0 < χ < 1/2 with speed constraints), monotonicity (for χ ≤ 0), and uniqueness/stability (for sufficiently large wave speeds exceeding a threshold c**).
AI.result: Existence is proven for χ ≤ 0 with speed c > c*_{χ,m,γ}, or for 0 < χ < 1/2 with c > 2. Monotonicity holds when χ ≤ 0. Uniqueness and stability hold when c > c**_{χ,m,α,γ}.
AI.conclusion: The paper significantly extends the theory of traveling waves for chemotaxis-logistic systems to general parameter regimes, establishing key qualitative properties beyond existence.

title: Three dimensional, spherically polarized magnetic fields
summary: Turbulence in the solar wind is characterized by Alfvénic fluctuations that exhibit spherical polarization, a geometric condition resulting in the nearly constant magnitude of the magnetic field. This property persists even during the largest field fluctuations, sometimes leading to local polarity reversals known as switchbacks. A longstanding question is whether three-dimensional smooth magnetic fields can simultaneously satisfy the constant-$|{\bf B}|$ constraint, and how such fields can be constructed analytically or numerically. Here we propose a new numerical method that allows to construct a magnetic field that is exactly spherically polarized, reproducing key features of solar wind fluctuations. Using this framework, we show that discontinuities are generically unavoidable in three-dimensional configurations. Fundamentally, this implies that field rotations cannot maintain exactly constant $|{\bf B}|$ in an arbitrarily large spatial domain. Rather, field rotations with constant magnitude can exist in limited regions of space separated by discontinuities where magnetic compressibility cannot be neglected. These results provide insights into the structure of solar wind turbulence and more generally into the nature of nonlinear magnetic fluctuations in plasmas.

AI.tldr: The authors develop a numerical method to construct exactly spherically polarized magnetic fields, demonstrating that discontinuities are unavoidable in 3D configurations with constant |B|, implying such fields can only exist in limited regions separated by discontinuities.
AI.motivation: To understand whether 3D smooth magnetic fields can simultaneously satisfy the constant-|B| (spherical polarization) constraint, and how such fields can be constructed, addressing a long-standing question about solar wind turbulence and switchbacks.
AI.method: A new numerical method is proposed to construct exactly spherically polarized magnetic fields that reproduce key features of solar wind fluctuations.
AI.result: Discontinuities are generically unavoidable in 3D configurations with constant |B|; field rotations with constant magnitude can only exist in limited spatial regions separated by discontinuities.
AI.conclusion: Insights into the structure of solar wind turbulence and the nature of nonlinear magnetic fluctuations in plasmas, highlighting where magnetic compressibility cannot be neglected.

title: Deep Wave Network for Modeling Multi-Scale Physical Dynamics
summary: Performance of deep learning models is strongly governed by architectural capacity, with width and depth as primary controls. However, in physical-science applications, models are often compared at a single fixed size or by separating accuracy and computational cost, which can be misleading since architectures exhibit different accuracy-cost scaling as width and depth vary. This issue is particularly relevant for U-Net-type encoder-decoder models, widely used for multi-scale gas, fluid, and plasma dynamics due to their ability to represent features across spatial scales. A U-Net constructs a multi-resolution representation via an encoder that progressively reduces spatial resolution, followed by a decoder that restores it for prediction. Skip connections link corresponding encoder and decoder features, preserving fine-scale information and improving optimization. In practice, U-Net width is routinely tuned, while depth is typically kept fixed (a set number of down/up-sampling stages with few convolutions per stage), limiting systematic exploration of depth for improving the accuracy-cost trade-off. We address this limitation by increasing effective depth through stacking multiple encoder-decoder "waves" in series, with skip connections both within and across waves to enable progressive cross-scale refinement. We call this architecture a Deep Wave Network (DW-Net). Training data, optimization, and schedules are kept identical across models. Instead of evaluating single configurations, we train multiple width variants of each architecture and compare accuracy vs. GPU time Pareto fronts. Across several 2D and 3D flow benchmarks, DW-Net models consistently improve the Pareto frontier over single-wave U-Nets, achieving higher accuracy at matched cost or similar accuracy at reduced cost, and reaching low-error regimes with up to 3x less training time under identical training settings.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Deep Wave Networks (DW-Nets), a U-Net variant that stacks multiple encoder-decoder "waves" with cross-wave skip connections to improve accuracy-cost trade-offs in physical-science applications. Compared to standard single-wave U-Nets, DW-Nets consistently achieve better Pareto frontiers across 2D and 3D flow benchmarks, reaching low-error regimes with up to 3x less training time.
AI.motivation: Standard practice in physical-science deep learning compares models at fixed sizes or separates accuracy from cost, which is misleading because architectures exhibit different accuracy-cost scaling. U-Net depth is typically kept fixed, limiting systematic exploration of depth for improving the accuracy-cost trade-off.
AI.method: DW-Net stacks multiple encoder-decoder "waves" in series, with skip connections both within and across waves, enabling progressive cross-scale refinement. Multiple width variants of each architecture are trained and compared via accuracy vs. GPU time Pareto fronts rather than single configurations.
AI.result: Across several 2D and 3D flow benchmarks, DW-Net models consistently improve the Pareto frontier over single-wave U-Nets, achieve higher accuracy at matched cost or similar accuracy at reduced cost, and reach low-error regimes with up to 3x less training time.
AI.conclusion: Increasing effective depth through stacked waves with cross-wave skip connections improves accuracy-cost trade-offs in U-Net architectures for physical-science applications, and evaluating models via Pareto fronts provides more meaningful comparisons than single-configuration benchmarks.

title: A third-order multi-moment cell-centered Lagrangian scheme for hydrodynamics with an accurate 2D nodal solver
summary: This paper presents a novel high-order cell-centered Lagrangian scheme for 2D compressible hydrodynamics by bridging the multi-moment constrained finite volume method (MCV) [16, 51, 52] with a nodal Riemann solver. This scheme (denoted by LMCV) not only maintains high-order accuracy as MCV but also inherits the conservation and robust properties of the nodal Riemann solver. On the one hand, the MCV employs and evolves both the point-values (PV) at cell vertexes and the volume-integrated averages (VIA) on computational mesh, which ensures the rigorous numerical conservation and establishes an adequate foundation for the computation of Lagrangian fluxes with high accuracy. On the other hand, we developed a 2D Riemann solver based on EUCCLHYD [24], it takes fully advantage of numerical formulations from high-order scheme and accomplishes the compatibility between the mesh movement and numerical fluxes. The main new features of the solver are the introduction of a new set of jump and balance conditions. The jump condition provides a high-accurate formulation linking the surface pressure of each cell to its nodal velocity, while the balance condition ensures nodal conservation and stabilizes the velocity field without losing accuracy. More intriguing is that our nodal solver can be regarded as a natural high-order extension of the HLLC and the HLLC-2D [41] solvers. The comparison between these solvers better demonstrates our innovative approach in addressing the difficulties encountered in constructing 2D high-order Lagrangian schemes. A variety of numerical experiments are carried out to illustrate the accuracy and robustness of the algorithm.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a high-order cell-centered Lagrangian scheme (LMCV) for 2D compressible hydrodynamics by combining the multi-moment constrained finite volume method with a nodal Riemann solver, achieving high accuracy, conservation, and robustness.
AI.motivation: To develop a high-order accurate Lagrangian scheme for 2D compressible hydrodynamics that addresses difficulties in constructing such schemes, particularly the compatibility between mesh movement and numerical fluxes.
AI.method: Bridges the multi-moment constrained finite volume method (MCV) with a nodal Riemann solver; uses both point-values at cell vertexes and volume-integrated averages; develops a 2D Riemann solver based on EUCCLHYD with new jump and balance conditions.
AI.result: The scheme maintains high-order accuracy, rigorous numerical conservation, and robust properties; can be viewed as a natural high-order extension of HLLC and HLLC-2D solvers; validated through numerical experiments.
AI.conclusion: The LMCV scheme successfully achieves high-order accuracy, conservation, and robustness for 2D Lagrangian hydrodynamics, offering a novel approach that extends existing Riemann solvers to higher order.

title: Asymptotics of Minimizers for Ginzburg--Landau-type Functionals in High Dimensions
summary: We investigate local minimizers of Ginzburg--Landau-type functionals in dimension $n\geq 3$ that satisfy logarithmic energy bounds, assuming the potential has a vacuum manifold with a finite fundamental group. We show that the normalized energy measures converge to an $(n-2)$-rectifiable measure associated with a stationary varifold, with quantized density determined by the homotopy classes of the vacuum manifold. Away from the support of the $(n-2)$-rectifiable measure, the minimizers converge strongly in $H^1_{\text{loc}}$ to a minimizing harmonic map, which is smooth outside an $(n-3)$-rectifiable singular set.

AI.tldr: The paper studies local minimizers of Ginzburg-Landau functionals in dimensions n≥3, showing that under logarithmic energy bounds and with a finite fundamental group vacuum manifold, the energy measures converge to quantized (n-2)-rectifiable varifolds, while minimizers converge to harmonic maps that are smooth except on an (n-3)-rectifiable singular set.
AI.motivation: To understand the asymptotic behavior of Ginzburg-Landau minimizers in higher dimensions (n≥3) when the potential's vacuum manifold has a finite fundamental group, extending prior results beyond the classical circle case.
AI.method: Analysis of local minimizers of Ginzburg-Landau-type functionals under logarithmic energy bounds, using geometric measure theory to study convergence of normalized energy measures and regularity theory for harmonic maps.
AI.result: Normalized energy measures converge to (n-2)-rectifiable measures associated with stationary varifolds, with quantized density determined by homotopy classes. Minimizers converge strongly in H¹_loc to a minimizing harmonic map, smooth outside an (n-3)-rectifiable singular set.
AI.conclusion: The work establishes a precise connection between Ginzburg-Landau minimizers and harmonic maps via rectifiable structures, generalizing known results to vacuum manifolds with nontrivial fundamental group in higher dimensions.

title: A physics-informed neural network approach to solve the spatially inhomogeneous electron Boltzmann equation
summary: The accurate determination of electron properties is fundamental to low-temperature plasma simulations, necessitating precise solutions to the spatially inhomogeneous electron Boltzmann equation (EBE). This work explores the use of physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) for obtaining solutions to the spatially one-dimensional (1D) EBE subject to a uniform electric field in atomic gases. Employing the two-term approximation, the resulting equation for the isotropic distribution is solved directly in kinetic energy space without the conventional transformation to total energy. This approach demonstrates the flexibility of the PINN framework in handling diverse equation formulations. To address the convergence difficulties associated with this class of kinetic equations, a new neural network architecture is introduced. It features a Fourier-feature input layer, adaptive activation functions, and a scaled multiplicative gating mechanism. It is demonstrated that this formulation preserves robust gradient flow throughout the network, which is critical for learning physically correct solutions. Benchmarking against reference data reveals that the present architecture achieves excellent agreement across both microscopic and macroscopic properties of the electrons. Furthermore, the architecture exhibits strong generalization across different gas types and a defined range of electric field strengths without requiring case-specific hyperparameter tuning. Ultimately, the excellent accuracy achieved here validates the applicability of the present PINN method.

AI.tldr: This paper applies physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) with a novel architecture (Fourier-feature input, adaptive activations, and scaled multiplicative gating) to solve the 1D electron Boltzmann equation under a uniform electric field, achieving excellent accuracy and strong generalization across gas types and field strengths without case-specific tuning.
AI.motivation: Accurate determination of electron properties is fundamental to low-temperature plasma simulations, requiring precise solutions to the spatially inhomogeneous electron Boltzmann equation (EBE).
AI.method: Uses PINNs with a new architecture including a Fourier-feature input layer, adaptive activation functions, and a scaled multiplicative gating mechanism. Solves the EBE directly in kinetic energy space under the two-term approximation without transformation to total energy.
AI.result: The architecture achieves excellent agreement with reference data for both microscopic and macroscopic electron properties. It generalizes well across different gas types and electric field strengths without case-specific hyperparameter tuning.
AI.conclusion: The excellent accuracy validates the applicability of the present PINN method for solving the spatially 1D EBE.

title: Angular Gausslets
summary: Gausslets are one of the few basis constructions for electronic structure that combine locality, orthonormality, variable resolution, and an accurate diagonal approximation for the electron-electron interaction, but the original construction is tied to one dimension. Radial gausslets extended this idea to atoms while leaving the angular degrees of freedom in spherical harmonics, so the atomic interaction remained only partially diagonal in the combined basis. Here we introduce generalized gausslets on the sphere and combine them shell by shell with radial gausslets to form an atom-centered basis in which the electron-electron interaction takes a two-index integral-diagonal form. The angular basis starts from localized spherical Gaussians and uses injection to make a low-$\ell$ spherical-harmonic subspace exact. Tests of the kinetic spectrum, low-$\ell$ Coulomb matrix elements, spherium, first-row Hartree--Fock calculations, and He exact diagonalization show systematic convergence with increasing angular resolution. We also develop DMRG methods for this basis, including compact MPOs, correlated small-space starting states, Givens-rotation transfers between nearby angular sizes, and embedded sampled variance extrapolation (ESVE). We show that this combination of ingredients can be used to solve the Be atom, with extrapolations in the number of angular functions but with fixed radial resolution, to within about 0.1 mH of the complete basis set limit exact energy. This shows that DMRG calculations of first row atoms which include both static and accurate dynamic correlation on the same footing are feasible.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces generalized gausslets on the sphere combined with radial gausslets to create an atom-centered basis where the electron-electron interaction takes a two-index integral-diagonal form. Tests show systematic convergence, and DMRG methods with this basis solve the Be atom to within ~0.1 mH of the exact energy.
AI.motivation: To extend gausslet basis constructions to three dimensions for atoms while maintaining locality, orthonormality, variable resolution, and a diagonal or near-diagonal electron-electron interaction representation.
AI.method: Generalized gausslets on the sphere are created from localized spherical Gaussians with injection to make low-ℓ spherical harmonics exact, then combined shell-by-shell with radial gausslets. DMRG methods are developed including compact MPOs, correlated starting states, Givens-rotation transfers, and embedded sampled variance extrapolation (ESVE).
AI.result: Tests of kinetic spectrum, low-ℓ Coulomb matrix elements, spherium, first-row Hartree-Fock calculations, and He exact diagonalization show systematic convergence. Be atom solved to within ~0.1 mH of the complete basis set limit exact energy.
AI.conclusion: DMRG calculations for first-row atoms that include both static and accurate dynamic correlation on the same footing are feasible using this combined gausslet basis.

title: Hamiltonian Interface Dynamics for Reduced-Order Optimization of Incompressible Mixing
summary: We develop a reduced-order framework for optimizing mixing in two-dimensional incompressible flows. Instead of optimizing the full transport PDE, the method maximizes the length of advected material interfaces, leading to a finite-dimensional Hamiltonian control problem based on parametrized stream functions. We derive the continuous adjoint equations and reduced gradients, and discretize the forward and adjoint dynamics with the implicit midpoint rule. The resulting discrete adjoint is algebraically consistent with the derivative of the fully discrete objective, up to the tolerance of the nonlinear midpoint solves. The approach applies to bounded two-dimensional domains with smooth finite-dimensional stream-function parametrizations. Numerical experiments on cellular-flow and Doswell frontogenesis benchmarks show that the optimized time-dependent Hamiltonians generate near-exponential interface stretching and substantially faster decay of the $\dot{H}^{-1}$ mix-norm, in contrast with the polynomial behavior observed for stationary flows. When evaluated on a common reference transport solver, the interface-based controls produce faster $\dot{H}^{-1}$ decay than a Eulerian Sobolev-norm optimizer under a matched setup, while substantially reducing computational cost. We also identify a limitation of the reduced model: increasing the control basis may further improve the interface-length objective without yielding proportional gains in $\dot{H}^{-1}$ mixing, confirming that interface length is an effective but not fully faithful proxy for mixing in geometrically complex regimes.

AI.tldr: The paper develops a reduced-order optimization framework for mixing in 2D flows by maximizing advected interface length, leading to a Hamiltonian control problem. The method achieves near-exponential interface stretching and faster mix-norm decay than stationary flows and Eulerian optimizers, but shows limitations as interface length is an imperfect mixing proxy in complex regimes.
AI.motivation: To develop a computationally efficient reduced-order framework for optimizing mixing in 2D incompressible flows, avoiding the high cost of optimizing the full transport PDE directly.
AI.method: Maximizes advected material interface length via finite-dimensional Hamiltonian control with parametrized stream functions, derives continuous adjoint equations and reduced gradients, and discretizes using the implicit midpoint rule with algebraically consistent discrete adjoints.
AI.result: Optimized time-dependent Hamiltonians produce near-exponential interface stretching and faster $\dot{H}^{-1}$ mix-norm decay than stationary flows. The method outperforms a Eulerian Sobolev-norm optimizer in decay speed with lower computational cost, though increasing control basis can improve interface length without proportional mixing gains.
AI.conclusion: Interface-length optimization is an effective but imperfect proxy for mixing; the reduced framework offers substantial computational savings but has limitations in geometrically complex regimes where interface length and mix-norm may decouple.

title: Stabilization by a background magnetic field: global well-posedness of the compressible isentropic ideal MHD equations with velocity damping
summary: We study the Cauchy problem for the three-dimensional isentropic compressible ideal (inviscid and non-resistive) magnetohydrodynamic equations with velocity damping on the periodic torus $\mathbb{T}^3$. The system admits a steady equilibrium consisting of a constant density $\barρ$ and a uniform background magnetic field $ω\in\mathbb{R}^3$. We prove that this equilibrium is nonlinearly stable. More precisely, we show that if the initial data are a sufficiently small perturbation of $(\barρ,\mathbf{0},ω)$ in the Sobolev space $H^N(\mathbb{T}^3)$ with $N\geq 6r+4$, and if $ω$ satisfies a Diophantine condition, then the system admits a unique global smooth solution. Moreover, the perturbations decay algebraically in time. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first global well-posedness result for the multi-dimensional isentropic compressible ideal MHD system. The proof reveals a hidden dissipation mechanism: although neither the density equation nor the magnetic field equation contains explicit diffusion or damping, the coupling between the velocity and the magnetic field through the background field $ω$, combined with a Diophantine--Poincaré inequality, generates effective dissipation for both the density perturbation and the magnetic field perturbation, which together with the velocity damping yields global regularity and time decay.

AI.tldr: This paper proves the nonlinear stability of steady equilibria for 3D compressible ideal MHD equations with velocity damping on a periodic torus, showing global smooth solutions exist for small perturbations and decay algebraically, using a new dissipation mechanism from coupling and Diophantine conditions.
AI.motivation: To establish the first global well-posedness result for multi-dimensional isentropic compressible ideal MHD systems, addressing the challenge that the density and magnetic field equations lack explicit diffusion or damping.
AI.method: The proof uses a Diophantine condition on the background magnetic field combined with a Diophantine–Poincaré inequality to reveal a hidden dissipation mechanism arising from the coupling between velocity and magnetic field, compensating for the lack of explicit diffusion in density and magnetic field equations.
AI.result: For small initial perturbations in H^N (N≥6r+4) and a Diophantine background magnetic field, the system has a unique global smooth solution, with perturbations decaying algebraically in time.
AI.conclusion: This is the first global well-posedness result for multi-dimensional isentropic compressible ideal MHD, demonstrating that coupling-induced effective dissipation can replace explicit diffusion in such systems.

title: Euler Singularities II: Interior Quadrupole Blow-Up for Smooth Axisymmetric Euler with Swirl in \texorpdfstring{$\mathbb R^3$}
summary:
We present a self-contained interior quadrupole mechanism for finite-time singularity formation in the axisymmetric three-dimensional incompressible Euler equations with swirl in the whole space. The construction is localized away from the axis. In local variables \[
  x=r-r_*(t),\qquad y=z, \] centered at a tracked radial point, the active vorticity and swirl profiles are \[
  G(x,y,t)\approx a(t)xy,
  \qquad
  Γ(x,y,t)\approx Γ_*(t)+\frac12 b(t)xy^2,
  \qquad Γ_*(t)>0. \] The first profile produces a positive interior Biot--Savart hyperbolic strain; the second profile makes the Euler source term in the equation for \(G=ω^θ/r\) regenerate the same quadrupole shape. The active quantity is the full four-quadrant quadrupole score, while a narrow diagonal sector is used only as a coercive subscore. We give the notation and the 5D recovery formula connecting the 3D axisymmetric variables to the lifted elliptic problem, construct explicit smooth decaying divergence-free data, verify their initial entry into the quadrupole bootstrap, prove the master propagation estimates, and derive the comparison system \[
  Q'(t)\ge cC(t),\qquad C'(t)\ge cQ(t)C(t),\qquad C(t)\ge κQ(t)^2. \] Consequently the tracked quadrupole score blows up in finite comparison time, and the strain lower bound gives blow-up of \(\norm{\nabla u(t)}_{L^\infty}\). All geometric and analytic constraints used by the construction are stated as named estimates: the interior quadrupole kernel sign expansion, source compatibility, swirl-jet amplification, full-score/coercive-subscore comparison, angular-profile defect persistence, radial-center tracking, neutral-jet hierarchy, and two-sided Dini bounds.
  This is Part II of a two-paper Euler series; Part I treats boundary blow-up in a periodic cylinder.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a self-contained interior quadrupole mechanism for finite-time singularity formation in the 3D axisymmetric Euler equations with swirl. It constructs explicit blow-up solutions, defines a quadrupole score that blows up in finite time, leading to infinite velocity gradient, and proves the result through a series of named estimates.
AI.motivation: To prove finite-time singularity formation in the 3D incompressible Euler equations with swirl, a long-standing open problem in fluid dynamics. The interior quadrupole mechanism avoids the axis and provides a self-contained construction.
AI.method: The method uses a tracked quadrupole mechanism with specific vorticity and swirl profiles (G ≈ a(t)xy, Γ ≈ Γ* + ½ b(t)xy²). It defines a quadrupole score Q(t) and a coercive subscore C(t), derives a comparison system of ODEs (Q' ≥ cC, C' ≥ cQC, C ≥ κQ²), and proves finite-time blow-up of the score, which implies blow-up of ‖∇u‖_L^∞.
AI.result: Finite-time singularity formation is proved: the quadrupole score blows up in finite comparison time, and the strain lower bound gives blow-up of the L^∞ norm of the velocity gradient. The construction satisfies all geometric and analytic constraints via named estimates.
AI.conclusion: The paper establishes an interior blow-up mechanism for the 3D axisymmetric Euler equations with swirl, complementing Part I's boundary blow-up in a periodic cylinder. The quadrupole bootstrap closes through a system of differential inequalities leading to finite-time singularity.

title: Transport of electrons in tangled magnetic fields
comment: 84 pages, 16 figures; accepted for publication in Space Science Reviews
summary: Cosmic magnetic fields are typically inhomogeneous and often highly tangled due to large-scale plasma flows, turbulence, and instabilities. If the variations in the magnetic field occur on scales that are large compared to the gyro-radius of the plasma electrons, the electrons are primarily confined to gyro-centre trajectories along the field lines. Therefore, in-situ electron measurements help us map out the connectivity of the magnetic field in space plasmas. Gyro-centre drifts, wave-particle interactions, trapping, and cross-field diffusion are processes related to field inhomogeneities and fluctuations; they have the potential to modify or even disrupt the transport of electrons along field lines. We introduce the basic principles of electron transport in tangled magnetic fields and review the creation of tangled fields through turbulence and instabilities as well as the modulation of parallel electron transport through kinetic instabilities. We then describe trapping and de-trapping effects in inhomogeneous magnetic fields, as well as electron diffusion and energisation across the magnetic field. The transport of electrons in tangled fields results from a complex interplay of plasma processes that occur on a broad range of scales. A combination of in-situ plasma measurements, remote-sensing plasma observations, and plasma theory and simulations is required to resolve this contemporary challenge to the fields of heliophysics and astrophysics.

AI.tldr: This paper reviews how tangled (inhomogeneous) cosmic magnetic fields affect electron transport, covering gyro-centre motion, trapping, diffusion, and energisation, and emphasizes the need for combined observational and theoretical approaches.
AI.motivation: To understand how inhomogeneous and tangled magnetic fields modulate electron transport in space plasmas, a complex multi-scale problem relevant to heliophysics and astrophysics.
AI.method: Review of basic principles of electron transport in tangled fields, including gyro-centre drifts, wave-particle interactions, trapping/de-trapping, cross-field diffusion, and kinetic instabilities.
AI.result: Electron transport results from a complex interplay of processes across multiple scales; parallel transport is modulated by kinetic instabilities, while perpendicular transport involves diffusion and energisation.
AI.conclusion: Resolving electron transport in tangled fields requires combining in-situ measurements, remote-sensing observations, and plasma theory/simulations.

title: Geant4 Optical Simulation without C++
summary: The plain text geometry description syntax in Geant4 has been extended to incorporate optical properties for bulk materials and surface interfaces. This extension enables users to configure and execute comprehensive optical simulations without writing C++ code, significantly lowering the learning curve and eliminating the need for frequent recompilation. In this paper, we detail the implementation of the new ":prop" and ":surf" tags and validate them through examples of key optical processes, including Cherenkov radiation, scintillation, Rayleigh scattering, and absorption. Furthermore, we provide a thorough demonstration of configuring complex optical boundaries using the UNIFIED model. These capabilities are contextualized through practical scenarios, showcasing the extension's potential for rapid prototyping and simulation studies.

AI.tldr: The paper extends Geant4's plain text geometry syntax with optical properties (":prop" and ":surf" tags), enabling users to run optical simulations (Cherenkov, scintillation, Rayleigh scattering, absorption) without C++ coding, validated through examples and demonstrated with the UNIFIED model for complex boundaries.
AI.motivation: To lower the learning curve and eliminate frequent recompilation by allowing users to configure optical simulations in Geant4 without writing C++ code.
AI.method: Extended Geant4's plain text geometry syntax with ":prop" (bulk material optical properties) and ":surf" (surface interface optical properties) tags; validated via examples of Cherenkov, scintillation, Rayleigh scattering, and absorption; demonstrated with UNIFIED model for complex optical boundaries.
AI.result: Successful implementation of the new tags; validation through key optical processes; demonstration of complex boundary configurations using the UNIFIED model.
AI.conclusion: The extension enables rapid prototyping and simulation studies for optical simulations in Geant4 without C++ coding, lowering barriers for users.

title: Steady Incremental Viscosity Splitting Method for solving the stationary Navier-Stokes equation
summary: We develop a novel and efficient iterative scheme for solving incompressible steady Navier-Stokes equations. The method is an adaptation of the Incremental Viscosity Splitting approximation for unsteady flows to steady equations. At each nonlinear iteration, the scheme requires solving an elliptic PDE for the velocity variable and a system with an SPD matrix for the pressure variable, which remains the same across all nonlinear iterations. The method can also be interpreted as an algebraic splitting approach. We prove boundedness and geometric convergence. Numerical tests illustrate the efficiency of the proposed algorithm.

AI.tldr: A novel iterative scheme for solving steady Navier-Stokes equations, adapted from Incremental Viscosity Splitting, requiring one elliptic PDE solve per nonlinear iteration and a fixed SPD pressure system, with proven boundedness and geometric convergence.
AI.motivation: To develop an efficient iterative solver for steady incompressible Navier-Stokes equations by adapting unsteady Incremental Viscosity Splitting (IVS) methods to the steady case.
AI.method: Adapts Incremental Viscosity Splitting approximation from unsteady to steady flows; at each nonlinear iteration, solves an elliptic PDE for velocity and a fixed SPD system for pressure; can also be interpreted as an algebraic splitting approach.
AI.result: Proved boundedness and geometric convergence of the proposed scheme; numerical tests demonstrate computational efficiency.
AI.conclusion: The proposed iterative scheme is efficient for solving steady Navier-Stokes equations, with theoretical guarantees and practical computational advantages due to the fixed SPD pressure system across iterations.

title: Global well-posedness and decay rates for the three dimensional incompressible active liquid crystals
summary: This paper investigates the global well-posedness and large-time behavior of 3D incompressible active liquid crystals under constant activity, modeled by a coupled system of forced incompressible Navier-Stokes equations for the velocity and a parabolic system for the $Q$-tensor order parameter. By employing refined commutator estimates, the existence and uniqueness of global strong solutions are proved for small initial data $(Q_0,u_0)\in H^{s+1}\times H^s$ $(s\geq 2)$ with activity $c>c_\star$, which improves a previous result in \cite{active-limit}. In addition, if the initial data further belong to $L^1$ and $s\geq 4$, we obtain a mixing decay estimate on $\|\partial^kQ(t)\|_{L^2}$ that combines both an extra exponential decay factor at a rate proportional to $(c-c_\star)Γ$ and the optimal algebraic decay rate that coincides with that of the heat kernel, where $k\leq s-1$. This result reveals that, in the high activity regime, active nematics become isotropic with an activity-dependent exponential convergence rate, and the estimate is stable in the infinite rotational viscosity limit, as $Γ\rightarrow 0$. Meanwhile, the sharp decay estimate on $\|\partial^ku(t)\|_{L^2}$ is also derived for $k\leq s-2$ with an additional initial assumption. The proof is established via a combination of the Green's function method and the time-weighted energy method. To the best of our knowledge, these results are the first reported for active/passive nematic liquid crystals within the Beris-Edwards framework, and the enhanced decay effect of the orientational field is essentially derived from the free energy. Furthermore, in the passive setting, our result implies the phase transition of thermotropic liquid crystals at high temperatures.

AI.tldr: This paper proves global well-posedness and enhanced decay estimates for 3D active liquid crystals under high activity, using Green's function and time-weighted energy methods.
AI.motivation: To improve previous existence results and reveal the enhanced decay effect of the orientational field in active nematics, particularly in the high activity regime.
AI.method: Refined commutator estimates for global strong solutions; Green's function method combined with time-weighted energy method for decay estimates.
AI.result: Existence and uniqueness for small initial data with activity c > c_*; mixing decay estimate on Q-tensor with exponential factor proportional to (c-c_*)Γ; sharp decay estimate on velocity; stability as Γ→0.
AI.conclusion: In high activity regimes, active nematics become isotropic with activity-dependent exponential convergence; results are first reported for Beris-Edwards framework, with implications for passive thermotropic liquid crystal phase transitions.

title: Nonlinear steepening of a fast magnetoacoustic wave in the vicinity of a coronal magnetic null point
comment: 15 pages, 7 figures; accepted by Phil. Trans. R. Soc. A, November 2025
summary: The interaction of a fast magnetoacoustic wave with a magnetic null point is studied in the context of the sympathetic flare phenomenon. Attention is paid to steepening the wave caused by the finite-amplitude effects in a non-uniform plasma environment. The null point is modelled by a potential magnetic configuration without a guiding field. The equilibrium plasma density and temperature are taken to be constant. The fast wave is excited by an impulsive point source outside the distance at which the local Alfvén and sound speeds are equal to each other. The incoming fast wave approaches the null point along the bisector of the magnetic configuration, i.e., across the local field. The fast-speed non-uniformity around the null point causes the refraction of the incident fast wave. However, the segment of the incoming wave, which approaches the null point is locally plane. The decrease in the fast speed towards the null point amplifies the nonlinear deformation of the incoming wave. Hence, the fast wave can become subject to nonlinear dissipation at a distance from the null point and not reach it.

AI.tldr: This paper studies how fast magnetoacoustic waves interact with magnetic null points in the context of solar sympathetic flares, finding that nonlinear steepening and dissipation can occur before the wave reaches the null point.
AI.motivation: To understand the interaction of fast magnetoacoustic waves with magnetic null points in relation to the sympathetic flare phenomenon, where one solar flare triggers another.
AI.method: The authors model a magnetic null point using a potential magnetic configuration with constant equilibrium plasma density and temperature. A fast wave is excited by an impulsive point source, and its propagation toward the null point along the bisector is analyzed, focusing on finite-amplitude nonlinear effects in the non-uniform plasma.
AI.result: The decreasing fast speed toward the null point amplifies nonlinear wave deformation, causing the wave to undergo nonlinear dissipation before reaching the null point. The incoming wave segment near the null point is locally plane despite refraction effects.
AI.conclusion: Fast magnetoacoustic waves can dissipate nonlinearly at a distance from the magnetic null point, potentially affecting sympathetic flare triggering mechanisms.

title: Interaction-controlled localization in one-dimensional chain: From edges to domain walls
summary: Using Hartree-Fock mean-field approach, we study the role of on-site ($U$) and extended ($V$) Hubbard interactions on the existence and evolution of edge modes in a half-filled Su-Schrieffer-Heeger (SSH) chain. We analyze the energy spectrum, local probability amplitudes, and site-resolved charge and spin density profiles across topological, critical, and trivial hopping regimes. We find that the localization of bound states is controlled by the ratio $2V/U$, with edge spin-density-wave modes for $U>2V$ and mid-chain charge-density-wave domain walls for $U<2V$, independent of band topology. These results establish the correlation-driven origin of localized states in finite one-dimensional chains.

AI.tldr: Using Hartree-Fock, this paper shows that edge modes in an SSH chain are controlled by the interaction ratio 2V/U rather than band topology, yielding spin-density-wave edges for U>2V and charge-density-wave domain walls for U<2V.
AI.motivation: To understand how on-site (U) and extended (V) Hubbard interactions affect the existence and evolution of edge modes in a finite SSH chain, and to determine whether these states are topologically protected or correlation-driven.
AI.method: Hartree-Fock mean-field approach applied to a half-filled SSH chain with both U and V interactions; analysis of energy spectrum, local probability amplitudes, and site-resolved charge/spin density profiles across topological, critical, and trivial hopping regimes.
AI.result: The localization of bound states is governed by the ratio 2V/U: edge spin-density-wave modes emerge for U>2V, while mid-chain charge-density-wave domain walls appear for U<2V, independent of the underlying band topology.
AI.conclusion: Localized states in finite one-dimensional chains are correlation-driven rather than topologically protected, with the interaction ratio 2V/U serving as the key control parameter.
