title: Superconvergence in finite element method by smoothing
summary: This paper develops a smoothing-based postprocessing method for superconvergence in finite element methods. The method applies a few smoothing iterations, such as damped Jacobi, Gauss-Seidel, or conjugate gradient, with initial guess being the current finite element solution embedded in an enriched finite element space. The resulting procedure is algebraic, easy to implement, and applicable to high-order and three-dimensional discretizations. For symmetric and positive-definite problems, we prove superconvergence of the smoothed solutions under additive and multiplicative smoothers. Effectiveness of the proposed method is demonstrated by numerical experiments for the Poisson, Maxwell, biharmonic and Helmholtz equations.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a smoothing-based postprocessing method that enhances finite element solution accuracy through algebraic smoothing iterations, achieving superconvergence for various PDEs.
AI.motivation: To develop an easy-to-implement, algebraic postprocessing technique that achieves superconvergence in finite element methods, applicable to high-order and 3D discretizations.
AI.method: Apply smoothing iterations (damped Jacobi, Gauss-Seidel, CG) with initial guess from enriched finite element space; prove superconvergence for SPD problems under additive/multiplicative smoothers.
AI.result: Proved superconvergence theoretically for symmetric positive-definite problems; demonstrated effectiveness on Poisson, Maxwell, biharmonic, and Helmholtz equations.
AI.conclusion: The algebraic, easy-to-implement smoothing postprocessing method achieves superconvergence and works across diverse PDEs and discretization types.

title: Recent progress in generalized Hamiltonian gradient flow: Singularities
summary:
This paper is a survey of the generalized Hamiltonian gradient flow (GHGF) framework for Hamilton-Jacobi equations, with an emphasis on the propagation of singularities and its connections to weak KAM theory, optimal transport and mean field control.
  In addition to reviewing the main ideas and known results, we present two new contributions. First, we provide a variational construction of generalized characteristics via a minimizing movement scheme; by taking the weak limit of approximate solutions and using Young measure compactness, we show that the limiting curve satisfies the generalized characteristic differential inclusion. Second, we lift the forward dynamics of strict singular characteristics to a semi-flow on the space of trajectories and study its invariant probability measures. We prove that the only invariant measures of the GHGF semi-flow that attain the critical value \(c[H]\) are precisely the projected Mather measures, thereby giving a new dynamical characterization of Mather's minimal measures as well as Mañé's critical value.
  Finally, we discuss a number of open problems that arise from the GHGF perspective, including questions on uniqueness of strict singular characteristics, rectifiability of cut loci, stability under perturbations, contact Hamiltonian systems, vanishing noise limits, and extensions to non-convex or low-regularity Hamiltonians. These problems highlight the deeper connections between singular dynamics, ergodic theory, optimal transport, and geometric analysis, and indicate directions for future research.

AI.tldr: This survey paper reviews the generalized Hamiltonian gradient flow (GHGF) framework for Hamilton-Jacobi equations, focusing on singularity propagation and connections to weak KAM theory, optimal transport, and mean field control. It contributes a variational construction of generalized characteristics via minimizing movement schemes and a new dynamical characterization of Mather's minimal measures as invariant measures of the GHGF semi-flow.
AI.motivation: To unify and extend the understanding of singularity propagation in Hamilton-Jacobi equations through the GHGF framework, bridging gaps between weak KAM theory, optimal transport, and mean field control, while providing new theoretical foundations for generalized characteristics and Mather measures.
AI.method: (1) Variational construction of generalized characteristics using minimizing movement schemes with Young measure compactness to obtain weak limits satisfying generalized characteristic differential inclusions. (2) Lifting strict singular characteristic dynamics to a semi-flow on trajectory space and analyzing invariant probability measures to characterize Mather measures.
AI.result: The limiting curve from the minimizing movement scheme satisfies the generalized characteristic differential inclusion. The only invariant measures of the GHGF semi-flow attaining the critical value c[H] are precisely the projected Mather measures, providing a new dynamical characterization of Mather's minimal measures and Mañé's critical value.
AI.conclusion: The GHGF perspective offers deep connections between singular dynamics, ergodic theory, optimal transport, and geometric analysis, with open problems including uniqueness of strict singular characteristics, rectifiability of cut loci, stability under perturbations, contact Hamiltonian systems, vanishing noise limits, and extensions to non-convex or low-regularity Hamiltonians.

title: Provable imitation learning for control of instability in partially-observed Vlasov--Poisson equations
summary: We consider the stabilization of Vlasov--Poisson plasma dynamics, a central control problem in nuclear fusion. Our focus is the gap between what an ideal controller would use and what experiments can actually observe: while optimal policy may rely on the full phase-space state, practical feedback is typically limited to sparse macroscopic diagnostics. We therefore study imitation learning methods that distill a fully observed expert policy into controllers operating only on macroscopic measurements. We show the stability guarantees of the learned policy, where the error floor depends on the minimal behavior cloning loss achievable under the observation constraints. We further characterize this minimal loss in terms of a notion of entropy that quantifies the complexity of the initial distribution. Our results demonstrates the theoretical feasibility of learning stabilizing feedback policies for kinetic plasma dynamics from macroscopic observations, and exhibits the adaptivity of the learning approach to low-complexity structures. Through extensive numerical experiments, we validate our theory and show that the learned policies can stabilize the system using only macroscopic observations, within a significantly longer time horizon than non-adaptive baseline controllers.

AI.tldr: This paper studies imitation learning for stabilizing Vlasov-Poisson plasma dynamics using only macroscopic observations, proving stability guarantees and characterizing the minimal behavior cloning loss through an entropy-based measure, with numerical validation showing learned policies outperform non-adaptive baselines.
AI.motivation: To bridge the gap between ideal full phase-space control and practical feedback limited to sparse macroscopic diagnostics in nuclear fusion plasma control.
AI.method: Imitation learning framework that distills a fully observed expert policy into controllers using only macroscopic measurements, with theoretical analysis of stability guarantees and minimal behavior cloning loss characterized by entropy of the initial distribution.
AI.result: Stability guarantees for learned policies with error floor depending on minimal behavior cloning loss; the minimal loss is characterized by an entropy measure of initial distribution complexity; learned policies stabilize the system using macroscopic observations over longer time horizons than non-adaptive baselines.
AI.conclusion: Theoretical feasibility of learning stabilizing feedback policies for kinetic plasma dynamics from macroscopic observations is established, with adaptivity to low-complexity structures demonstrated both theoretically and numerically.

title: An Adaptive Finite Element Method Based on Generalized Barycentric Coordinates
summary: This work derives a posteriori error estimate of polygonal finite element methods based on Wachspress barycentric coordinates. In particular, we prove that the classical residual-based a posteriori error estimator is both an upper and lower bounds for the discretization error. The analysis relies a Scott-Zhang type interpolation and homogeneity arguments for rational functions on polygonal elements. Numerical experiments on square and L-shaped domains demonstrate the effectiveness of the adaptive algorithm.

AI.tldr: This paper derives and validates a posteriori error estimates for polygonal finite element methods using Wachspress coordinates, proving the residual-based error estimator bounds the discretization error, with numerical tests confirming the adaptive algorithm's effectiveness.
AI.motivation: To establish rigorous a posteriori error estimates for polygonal finite element methods, enabling reliable adaptive mesh refinement for complex geometries.
AI.method: The authors derive residual-based a posteriori error estimators using Scott-Zhang type interpolation and homogeneity arguments for rational functions on polygonal elements with Wachspress barycentric coordinates.
AI.result: The classical residual-based a posteriori error estimator is proven to be both an upper and lower bound for the discretization error. Numerical experiments on square and L-shaped domains validate the adaptive algorithm.
AI.conclusion: The proposed error estimator is reliable and efficient for adaptive polygonal finite element methods, extending classical FEM error analysis to polygonal discretizations.

title: Traveling-wave behavior for Fisher-KPP equations in the hyperbolic space
summary: We study the Cauchy problem in the hyperbolic space for the heat equation with a Fisher-KPP type forcing term. Depending on the relative strength of diffusion, measured by the infimum of the spectrum of the Laplace-Beltrami operator, as compared to the growth due to the forcing term, solutions may propagate or vanish as time passes. We prove new results concerning this dichotomy that include the critical case where diffusion and reaction are of the same order. If the initial datum possesses some symmetry (invariance under a cohomogeneity one subgroup of the group of isometries of the hyperbolic space), the problem reduces to a unidimensional one. In the case of propagation, the solution to this unidimensional problem converges in shape to an Euclidean traveling wave of minimal speed in an appropriate moving frame. The choice of this frame depends on the subgroup of isometries (elliptic, hyperbolic or parabolic) under which the initial datum is invariant. In contrast with the Euclidean case, the asymptotic spreading speed (in due coordinates) depends on the dimension, while the coefficient of the logarithmic correction in the location of the front does not, no matter the underlying isometry.

AI.tldr: This paper studies the heat equation with Fisher-KPP nonlinearity in hyperbolic space, establishing conditions for solution propagation vs. vanishing, and analyzing asymptotic front behavior under symmetry reductions.
AI.motivation: To understand how geometry (hyperbolic space vs. Euclidean space) affects the propagation-vanishing dichotomy and asymptotic spreading behavior of reaction-diffusion equations, particularly in the critical case where diffusion and reaction are of the same order.
AI.method: The authors use spectral analysis of the Laplace-Beltrami operator on hyperbolic space, symmetry reduction via cohomogeneity one subgroups of isometries (elliptic, hyperbolic, parabolic), and moving frame analysis to reduce the problem to a unidimensional traveling wave problem.
AI.result: New results on the propagation-vanishing dichotomy including the critical case; under symmetry, the solution converges to a minimal-speed Euclidean traveling wave; asymptotic spreading speed depends on dimension, but the logarithmic correction coefficient in front location does not depend on the underlying isometry type.
AI.conclusion: Hyperbolic geometry significantly alters the asymptotic behavior compared to Euclidean space: dimension affects the spreading speed, while the logarithmic correction is geometrically universal across isometry subgroups.

title: Convergence analysis of Schwarz-like methods for degenerate elliptic-parabolic equations
summary: Convergence is proven for Schwarz-like methods applied to degenerate elliptic-parabolic equations with a $p$-structure. This family of PDEs, e.g., arises when modelling nonlinear diffusion processes. The Schwarz-like approximation methods are based on decomposing the space-time domain into overlapping subdomains, which enables parallel implementations. The methods are derived by introducing a pseudo-time component and applying time integrators of splitting type, which are time stepped towards infinity. This approach of decomposing the space-time domain is related to Schwarz waveform relaxation methods, but the methods considered here have the advantage that they can be proven to converge when applied to nonlinear parabolic, or even degenerate elliptic-parabolic, PDEs. We prove convergence by deriving a nonlinear framework based on the abstract theory for monotone operators and the existence theory for degenerate elliptic-parabolic equations.

AI.tldr: This paper proves convergence of Schwarz-like domain decomposition methods for degenerate elliptic-parabolic equations with p-structure, using monotone operator theory and pseudo-time stepping towards infinity.
AI.motivation: To establish provable convergence guarantees for Schwarz-like methods applied to nonlinear parabolic and degenerate elliptic-parabolic PDEs, which arise in nonlinear diffusion modeling.
AI.method: Space-time domain decomposition into overlapping subdomains; introduction of pseudo-time component; splitting-type time integrators stepped to infinity; nonlinear framework based on monotone operator theory and existence theory for degenerate elliptic-parabolic equations.
AI.result: Convergence is proven for Schwarz-like methods applied to degenerate elliptic-parabolic equations with p-structure.
AI.conclusion: The proposed Schwarz-like methods, related to Schwarz waveform relaxation, can be proven convergent for nonlinear parabolic and degenerate elliptic-parabolic PDEs, offering an advantage over standard waveform relaxation approaches.

title: A scalar field equation on hyperbolic space with indefinite sign nonlinearity
summary:
In this article, we study threshold phenomena for the semilinear double-power elliptic equation $$-Δ_{\mathbb{B}^N} u - λu = |u|^{p-1}u - |u|^{q-1}u, \quad u \in H^1(\mathbb{B}^N),$$
  on the hyperbolic space $\mathbb{B}^N$ for $N \ge 3$. For parameters $1 < p \le 2^*-1$ (though we occasionally allow for supercritical exponents) and $q > 0$, we seek to identify the optimal spectral regimes for $λ\in \mathbb{R}$ that delineate the existence and non-existence of positive-energy solutions. We achieve a complete resolution of these thresholds across all exponent configurations: $p < q$, $0 < q < 1 < p$, and $1 < q < p$. Our results demonstrate that the boundary separating these regimes is governed by an explicit critical spectral parameter, which depends on $p$, $q$, and $N$ in the regime where $p < q$, but depends solely on $N$ in the remaining cases.

AI.tldr: This paper studies threshold phenomena for a semilinear double-power elliptic equation on hyperbolic space, identifying optimal spectral regimes for existence/non-existence of positive-energy solutions across all exponent configurations.
AI.motivation: To identify the optimal spectral regimes (for parameter λ) that delineate existence vs. non-existence of positive-energy solutions for a double-power elliptic equation on hyperbolic space, achieving complete resolution of thresholds across all exponent configurations.
AI.method: The authors analyze the semilinear double-power elliptic equation on hyperbolic space B^N (N≥3), considering different exponent configurations (p<q, 0<q<1<p, 1<q<p) and identifying critical spectral parameters that govern the boundary between existence and non-existence regimes.
AI.result: Complete resolution of thresholds across all exponent configurations; the boundary is governed by an explicit critical spectral parameter that depends on p, q, and N when p<q, but depends solely on N in the remaining cases.
AI.conclusion: The study achieves a complete classification of threshold phenomena for the double-power elliptic equation on hyperbolic space, with the critical spectral parameter's dependence structure varying by exponent regime.

title: Update-Magnitude State Redistribution (UM-SRD): A Shut-off Extension of Weighted SRD for Cut-Cell Methods
summary: Berger & Giuliani (2024) developed a provably stable weighted state redistribution (SRD) algorithm for cut-cell meshes. A key limitation of their method is that, although flux redistribu- tion naturally vanishes when updates are small, SRD continuously applies redistribution even when the flux balance is zero, preventing exact steady-state preservation and potentially in- troducing unnecessary dissipation in smooth regions. This work introduces Update-Magnitude State Redistribution (UM-SRD), which blends the SRD operator with the identity operator via a smooth, locally-defined scalar indicator of the finite-volume update magnitude. UM-SRD preserves conservation and reduces exactly to the base scheme when the finite-volume update is exactly zero in a small-cell neighborhood. For a one-dimensional model problem with a single small cut cell, we prove UM-SRD is total variation diminishing under the same CFL condition as the base upwind scheme, show the local truncation error modification is higher-order in smooth regions with the unnormalized indicator, and show that the normalized implementation pre- serves first-order accuracy. Numerical experiments demonstrate convergence toward first order on smooth 1D and 2D advection tests, confirm shut-off behaviour, verify non-oscillatory proper- ties, provide numerical evidence that UM-SRD stabilizes the base scheme near a small cut cell where the base scheme diverges, and confirm exact steady-state preservation. The algorithm reuses existing weighted SRD infrastructure, adding only a local blending mechanism, making it practical for cut-cell finite-volume codes.

AI.tldr: UM-SRD is an improved state redistribution method for cut-cell meshes that blends redistribution with the identity operator based on update magnitude, preserving steady states exactly and reducing dissipation while maintaining stability.
AI.motivation: Existing weighted SRD continuously redistributes even when flux balance is zero, preventing exact steady-state preservation and introducing unnecessary dissipation in smooth regions.
AI.method: UM-SRD blends the SRD operator with the identity operator via a smooth, locally-defined scalar indicator of the finite-volume update magnitude, reusing existing SRD infrastructure with only a local blending mechanism.
AI.result: Proven TVD under same CFL as base upwind scheme; higher-order local truncation error modification in smooth regions (unnormalized indicator); first-order accuracy preserved (normalized); numerical experiments confirm convergence, shut-off, non-oscillatory behavior, stabilization, and exact steady-state preservation.
AI.conclusion: UM-SRD addresses the steady-state preservation and unnecessary dissipation limitations of prior SRD methods while maintaining all stabilizing properties, and is practical for existing cut-cell finite-volume codes.

title: Normalized solutions to a class of Kirchhoff type equations with a logarithmic perturbation
summary: This paper is devoted to the study of normalized solutions to the Kirchhoff type equation with a logarithmic perturbation\[-\left(a+b\int_{\mathbb{R}^3}|\nabla u|^2 \,\mathrm{d}x \right) Δu=λu+|u|^{p-2}u+u\log u^2,\quad x \in\mathbb{R}^3, \]under the normalized constraint $\int_{\mathbb{R}^3} u^2 \,\mathrm{d}x = c^2$, where $a,b>0$, $2<p\leq 6$, $c>0$ is a constant, and $λ\in\mathbb{R}$ emerges as a Lagrange multiplier which is not a priori known. A unified variational framework is developed based on Orlicz spaces together with the Pohozaev constraint method and refined fiber map analysis. For $2<p<\frac{14}{3}$ or $p=\frac{14}{3}$ with small mass, the energy functional is bounded from below and admits a positive radial ground state minimizer. For $\frac{14}{3}<p<6$, where the energy functional is unbounded from below, we establish the existence of two normalized solutions for small mass: a ground state $u_c^+$ obtained via local minimization, and a second solution $u_c^-$ obtained via minimization on the negative component of the Pohozaev manifold. For the Sobolev critical case $p=6$, we construct a ground state solution and, under a technical condition on the parameters, a second solution by introducing a proper auxiliary functional and precise energy estimates with Aubin-Talenti bubbles. Asymptotically as $c\to0^+$, the $L^{2}$ norm of the gradient of ground state solution vanishes for $2<p\le6$. Surprisingly, for $\frac{14}{3}<p<6$, the $L^{2}$ norm of the gradient of the second solution diverges to infinity as $c\to 0^+$, while for $p=6$ it concentrates around the Aubin-Talenti bubble with energy converging to the energy level of the corresponding critical Kirchhoff equation.

AI.tldr: This paper studies normalized solutions to a Kirchhoff-type equation with logarithmic perturbation in ℝ³, developing a unified variational framework using Orlicz spaces, Pohozaev constraints, and fiber map analysis. It establishes existence of ground states for 2<p<14/3 or p=14/3 with small mass, two solutions for 14/3<p<6 (a ground state and a second solution), and ground state plus second solution for the critical case p=6, with detailed asymptotic analysis as c→0⁺.
AI.motivation: To study normalized solutions (fixed L² mass) of Kirchhoff-type equations with logarithmic nonlinearity, which arise in physics (e.g., string vibrations) and have not been systematically studied under the normalized constraint, especially regarding existence, multiplicity, and asymptotic behavior across different exponent regimes.
AI.method: A unified variational framework combining Orlicz spaces (to handle logarithmic perturbations), Pohozaev constraint method, and refined fiber map analysis. For the critical case p=6, auxiliary functionals and Aubin-Talenti bubble techniques are used.
AI.result: For 2<p<14/3 or p=14/3 with small mass: existence of a positive radial ground state. For 14/3<p<6: two solutions (local minimizer and solution on negative Pohozaev manifold). For p=6: ground state and a second solution under technical conditions. As c→0⁺, gradient L² norm of ground state vanishes for all 2<p≤6, but for the second solution it diverges (14/3<p<6) or concentrates at the Aubin-Talenti bubble (p=6).
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a comprehensive analysis of normalized solutions for Kirchhoff equations with logarithmic nonlinearity, revealing rich bifurcation phenomena depending on the exponent p and mass parameter c, including novel asymptotic divergence behavior for second solutions.

title: Finding accurate eigenvalues and eigenvectors of positive semi-definite matrices given a subspace
summary: We revisit a classical problem in numerical linear algebra: given an $k$-dimensional subspace $\mathcal{Q}$ that approximates the leading eigenspace of an $n\times n$ positive semi-definite matrix $A$, the goal is to extract high-accuracy eigenvalues. The Rayleigh-Ritz (RR) method is the standard algorithm for the task, which has been shown to be optimal in several ways (when $A$ is symmetric, not necessarily positive semi-definite $A\succeq 0$). In this paper, we show that when $A \succeq 0$, alternative methods can outperform RR, while having the same computational complexity, that is, the main cost is in computing $AQ$, plus an $O(nk^2)$ term. In particular, we advocate the use of Nystr{ö}m's method, showing that the approximate eigenvalues always have higher accuracy than RR, and the improvement can be arbitrarily large. The difference is significant, especially when $A$ has a fast-decaying spectrum. A similar improvement is numerically observed for the purpose of approximating the leading eigenvectors. In contrast, when the target eigenvalues are the trailing ones, the situation is reversed, and the Nystr{ö}m method performs poorly; we suggest a remedy for this situation.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that Nyström's method outperforms Rayleigh-Ritz for extracting high-accuracy eigenvalues from a subspace approximating the leading eigenspace of a positive semi-definite matrix, with identical computational complexity.
AI.motivation: Revisit the classical problem of extracting high-accuracy eigenvalues from an approximate eigenspace, questioning whether Rayleigh-Ritz (RR) is truly optimal when the matrix is positive semi-definite, and seeking alternatives that can achieve better accuracy.
AI.method: Proposes using Nyström's method instead of Rayleigh-Ritz for eigenvalue extraction from an approximate subspace, with computational cost of computing AQ plus O(nk²).
AI.result: Nyström's method always produces higher accuracy eigenvalues than RR, with improvements that can be arbitrarily large, especially for matrices with fast-decaying spectra. Similar improvements observed for eigenvectors numerically. However, for trailing eigenvalues, Nyström performs poorly.
AI.conclusion: When extracting leading eigenvalues from a positive semi-definite matrix, Nyström's method is superior to Rayleigh-Ritz at the same computational cost, but a remedy is needed for trailing eigenvalue extraction.

title: Non-uniqueness for a differential equation and a proof by ChatGPT
summary:
Let $f(t,x),M(t,x)\in C([0,1]^2)$ with $M(t,x)>0$. We consider differential equations of the form \[
  \frac{\partial f}{\partial t}(t,x)=\frac{M(t,x)f(t,x)-M(t,0)f(t,0)}{x},\quad x>0. \] For a fixed positive weight $M$, we ask whether the condition $f(0,x)=0$ forces $f\equiv 0$. We show the answer is negative for smooth functions: there exist $f(t,x),M(t,x)\in C^{\infty}([0,1]^2)$ with $f(0,x)=0$, $f(t,0)\not\equiv 0$, and $M(t,x)>0$ satisfying the above equation. However, we show that for a large class of $M(t,x)$, the equation does have uniqueness. We relate this to uniqueness/non-uniqueness theorems for weighted Laplace transforms. A key example originated in an output by ChatGPT-5.5-Pro, and we include a discussion of its output as well as a complete proof.

AI.tldr: This paper studies a class of differential equations with positive weight M(t,x) and examines whether the condition f(0,x)=0 forces f≡0. It shows non-uniqueness can occur for smooth functions but establishes uniqueness for a large class of weights, linking the results to weighted Laplace transforms and analyzing an example from ChatGPT-5.5-Pro.
AI.motivation: To understand uniqueness properties of a specific differential equation involving a positive weight function M(t,x), and to determine conditions under which the initial condition f(0,x)=0 uniquely determines the solution.
AI.method: The authors analyze the differential equation analytically, constructing counterexamples with smooth functions to show non-uniqueness, and proving uniqueness for certain classes of M(t,x). They connect the problem to weighted Laplace transform theory and include a case study of an output from ChatGPT-5.5-Pro.
AI.result: (1) Non-uniqueness: There exist smooth f,M with f(0,x)=0, f(t,0)≠0, M>0 satisfying the equation. (2) Uniqueness: For a large class of M(t,x), uniqueness holds. (3) The problem is linked to weighted Laplace transforms.
AI.conclusion: The initial condition f(0,x)=0 does not always force f≡0; smooth counterexamples exist. However, under certain conditions on the weight M, uniqueness can be restored. The findings are connected to uniqueness/non-uniqueness in weighted Laplace transforms.

title: BDF2-type integrator for Landau-Lifshitz-Gilbert equation in micromagnetics: a-priori error estimates
summary: We consider the Landau-Lifshitz-Gilbert equation (LLG), which models time-dependent micromagnetic phenomena. We analyze a fully discrete scheme that combines first-order finite elements in space with a BDF2 method in time. The method requires the solution of only one linear system of equations per time step and does not enforce the pointwise unit-length constraint of the magnetization. While unconditional weak convergence has been analyzed in an earlier work, we now prove optimal-order convergence rates under sufficient regularity assumptions on the exact solution and the external field. In combination with our previous work, this establishes the first higher-order-in-time and linear integrator that converges both to weak and strong solutions of LLG. Numerical experiments confirm first-order convergence in space and second-order convergence in time.

AI.tldr: This paper proves optimal-order convergence rates for a BDF2 finite element scheme for the Landau-Lifshitz-Gilbert equation, achieving first-order in space and second-order in time with only one linear system solve per time step.
AI.motivation: To establish optimal convergence rates for a linear, higher-order-in-time integrator for LLG that has already shown unconditional weak convergence, filling the gap between weak and strong convergence analysis.
AI.method: Combines first-order finite elements in space with BDF2 time discretization, requiring one linear system solve per step without enforcing the unit-length constraint; convergence analysis uses regularity assumptions on the exact solution and external field.
AI.result: Proves optimal-order convergence rates (first-order in space, second-order in time) under sufficient regularity assumptions, confirmed by numerical experiments.
AI.conclusion: This is the first higher-order-in-time, linear integrator that converges to both weak and strong solutions of LLG.

title: Renormalized Solution for the Nonlinear Parabolic Problem with Lower Order Terms
summary:
In this paper, we consider the following problem:
  \[
  \begin{cases}
  -\nabla\cdot A(x,u,\nabla u) + H(x,u,\nabla u) = f(x), & x \in Ω,
  u = 0, & x \in \partial Ω,
  \end{cases}
  \]
  in a bounded open set \( Ω\subset \mathbb{R}^N \). We have established certain gradient estimates and proved the existence of a renormalized solution for the equation.

AI.tldr: This paper establishes gradient estimates and proves the existence of renormalized solutions for a nonlinear elliptic boundary value problem with a general differential operator in a bounded domain.
AI.motivation: To establish existence results for nonlinear elliptic equations where classical solutions may not exist, by proving the existence of renormalized solutions under gradient estimates.
AI.method: The authors consider a nonlinear elliptic equation with a divergence-form operator and a lower-order term, and apply functional analysis techniques to derive gradient estimates, which are then used to prove existence of a renormalized solution.
AI.result: Certain gradient estimates are established, and the existence of a renormalized solution for the equation is proved.
AI.conclusion: The existence of renormalized solutions for the given nonlinear elliptic problem is confirmed, supported by gradient estimates.

title: Explicit Two-Sided Eigenvalue Bounds for Schrödinger Operators with Singular Potentials via Finite Element Method
comment: 44 pages, 4 figures. The source includes the appendices and uses the standard LaTeX article class for arXiv compatibility
summary: We present, to the best of our knowledge, the first numerical algorithm for explicit, computable two-sided eigenvalue bounds for Schrödinger operators H = -Delta + V on R^N, N = 2,3, in the presence of both an unbounded potential and an unbounded domain. "Explicit" here means that all constants and ingredients are derived in closed form from the mesh, the potential, and a small set of explicit inequalities (Payne-Weinberger, Hardy, and explicit bounded-domain Sobolev embeddings); the conversion to fully verified(IEEE-754-safe, interval-arithmetic) enclosures is a separate verification step and is left for future work. In particular, singular attractive potentials of Coulomb type, V(x) = -Z/|x|, which model the hydrogen atom and the H_2^+ molecular ion, are covered by the theory. The method combines domain truncation to a bounded domain D(R) containing {|x| <= R} with an extension of Liu's Composite Enriched Crouzeix-Raviart (CECR) finite element method to sign-indefinite potentials. Upper bounds come from the standard conforming Galerkin method; lower bounds come from the CECR construction, whose gap to the exact eigenvalue closes as the mesh is refined. Numerical experiments on the 2D single- and two-centred Coulomb potentials and on the 3D hydrogen atom and H_2^+ molecular ion illustrate the algorithm and confirm the predicted convergence.

AI.tldr: First numerical algorithm for explicit, computable two-sided eigenvalue bounds for Schrödinger operators with unbounded potentials and domains, including Coulomb potentials (hydrogen atom, H₂⁺). Combines domain truncation with CECR finite element method, yielding both upper and lower bounds that converge with mesh refinement.
AI.motivation: To provide explicit, computable two-sided eigenvalue bounds for Schrödinger operators with unbounded potentials and domains, particularly for singular attractive Coulomb potentials modeling hydrogen atom and H₂⁺ molecular ion, which previous methods could not handle.
AI.method: Combines domain truncation (D(R) containing {|x| ≤ R}) with Liu's Composite Enriched Crouzeix-Raviart (CECR) finite element method extended to sign-indefinite potentials. Upper bounds from standard conforming Galerkin method; lower bounds from CECR construction. Uses explicit inequalities (Payne-Weinberger, Hardy, bounded-domain Sobolev embeddings) with constants derived in closed form.
AI.result: Numerical experiments on 2D single- and two-centered Coulomb potentials and 3D hydrogen atom and H₂⁺ molecular ion confirm the algorithm works and converges as predicted.
AI.conclusion: First demonstrated method for explicit two-sided eigenvalue bounds on Schrödinger operators with unbounded potentials/domains, covering Coulomb-type singular potentials. The gap between upper and lower bounds closes with mesh refinement, and verification (interval arithmetic) is left for future work.

title: Existence of detonation wave solutions to the piston problem for the Zeldovich-von Neumann-D{ö}ring combustion model
summary: In this paper, we study detonation wave solutions to one-dimensional piston problem for the Zeldovich-von Neumann-D{ö}ring (ZND) combustion model with a one-step exothermic chemical reaction. As a special type of shock wave, the position of the detonation wave is unknown, which make our model to be a free boundary problem.~The global existence of detonation wave solutions to this free boundary problem is proved. Compared with previous result~\cite{Lai}, we do not impose any dissipation conditions on the equations and the boundaries.

AI.tldr: This paper proves global existence of detonation wave solutions to the one-dimensional piston problem for the ZND combustion model with a one-step exothermic chemical reaction, treated as a free boundary problem, without imposing dissipation conditions on the equations and boundaries.
AI.motivation: To prove global existence of detonation wave solutions for the ZND combustion model in a free boundary problem setting, relaxing previous assumptions about dissipation conditions on the equations and boundaries.
AI.method: The paper studies detonation wave solutions to the one-dimensional piston problem for the ZND combustion model with a one-step exothermic chemical reaction. The detonation wave position is unknown, making it a free boundary problem.
AI.result: The global existence of detonation wave solutions to this free boundary problem is proved.
AI.conclusion: Compared with previous results, this work does not impose any dissipation conditions on the equations and the boundaries.

title: Simultaneous CNN Approximation on Manifolds with Applications to Boundary Value Problems
summary: This paper develops convolutional neural network (CNN) methods for simultaneous approximation and elliptic boundary value problems on compact Riemannian manifolds. We establish simultaneous Sobolev approximation results for single- and multichannel CNNs, showing that manifold functions and their derivatives can be approximated with rates governed by the intrinsic dimension and the smoothness gap, rather than by the ambient dimension, thereby mitigating the curse of dimensionality. Building on this approximation theory, we propose a physics-informed CNN (PICNN) framework specially designed for boundary value problems. The main numerical issue is a boundary-norm mismatch: standard PINNs usually impose boundary data through low-order, often L2-type, penalties, whereas elliptic stability requires Sobolev trace control. We address this by introducing a spectral boundary loss based on the boundary Laplace-Beltrami operator, which represents trace errors as weighted frequency energies and relates truncation error to boundary eigenvalue decay. This avoids smooth auxiliary constructions required by exact boundary enforcement and singular double integrals arising in Sobolev-Slobodeckij penalties, while enabling implementations based on Fast Fourier Transforms (FFTs) or precomputed spectral bases on structured boundaries. Numerical experiments demonstrate improved accuracy, convergence, and stability over standard PINNs.

AI.tldr: This paper develops CNN methods for approximating functions and solving elliptic PDEs on Riemannian manifolds, proving Sobolev approximation rates that avoid the curse of dimensionality. It introduces a physics-informed CNN (PICNN) framework with a spectral boundary loss based on the boundary Laplace-Beltrami operator to resolve the boundary-norm mismatch in standard PINNs, enabling efficient FFT-based implementation and showing improved accuracy and stability over standard PINNs.
AI.motivation: To develop CNN-based approximation and PDE solution methods on Riemannian manifolds that mitigate the curse of dimensionality, and to address the boundary-norm mismatch issue in physics-informed neural networks where standard L2 penalty does not align with the Sobolev trace control needed for elliptic stability.
AI.method: The authors establish simultaneous Sobolev approximation theory for single- and multichannel CNNs on compact Riemannian manifolds. They propose a PICNN framework with a spectral boundary loss using the boundary Laplace-Beltrami operator, representing trace errors as weighted frequency energies, enabling FFT-based or precomputed spectral basis implementation on structured boundaries.
AI.result: Numerical experiments show improved accuracy, convergence, and stability over standard PINNs for elliptic boundary value problems on manifolds.
AI.conclusion: The proposed spectral boundary loss effectively resolves the boundary-norm mismatch, and the CNN-based framework achieves dimension-robust approximation rates, making it a promising approach for PDEs on manifolds.

title: Neural Discovery of Strichartz Extremizers
summary: Strichartz inequalities are a cornerstone of the modern theory of dispersive PDEs, but their extremizers are known explicitly only in a handful of sharp cases. The non-convexity of the underlying functional makes the problem hard, and to our knowledge no systematic numerical attack has been attempted. We propose a simple neural-network-based pipeline that searches for extremizers as critical points of the Strichartz ratio, and apply it in three settings. First, on the Schrödinger group we recover the Gaussian extremizers of Foschi and Hundertmark--Zharnitsky in dimensions $d=1,2$ to within $10^{-3}$ relative error, with no analytical prior. Second, on $59$ further admissible pairs in $d=1$ where the answer is conjectural, the method consistently finds Gaussians, supporting the conjecture that Gaussians are the universal extremizers in the admissible range. Third, on the critical Airy--Strichartz inequality at $γ=1/q$, where existence is open, the optimization does not converge to any $L^2$ profile: instead, the iterates organize themselves as mKdV breathers $B(0,\cdot;α,1,0,0)$ with growing internal frequency $α$, and the discovered ratio approaches the Frank--Sabin universal lower bound $\widetilde A_{q,r}$ from below with a power-law gap $\simα^{-0.9}$. We confirm the same picture with an independent Hermite-basis ansatz. We propose a precise conjecture: the supremum equals $\widetilde A_{q,r}$ and is approached, but not attained, along the breather family. The pipeline thus serves both as a validator on known cases and as a discovery tool when no extremizer exists.

AI.tldr: This paper uses a neural-network-based pipeline to search for extremizers of Strichartz inequalities, recovering known Gaussian extremizers (within 10⁻³ error), supporting the conjecture that Gaussians are universal extremizers in 59 admissible pairs, and discovering that the critical Airy-Strichartz inequality has no L² extremizer but is approached by mKdV breathers with a power-law gap.
AI.motivation: Strichartz inequalities are fundamental to dispersive PDEs, but their extremizers are known only in a few sharp cases due to the non-convexity of the functional, and no systematic numerical approach had been attempted.
AI.method: A neural-network-based pipeline that searches for extremizers as critical points of the Strichartz ratio, applied to the Schrödinger group (d=1,2), 59 admissible pairs in d=1, and the critical Airy-Strichartz inequality.
AI.result: - Recovered Gaussian extremizers to within 10⁻³ relative error in dimensions 1,2. - Gaussians found consistently across 59 admissible pairs, supporting the universal extremizer conjecture. - For Airy-Strichartz, no L² convergence; iterates organize as mKdV breathers with growing internal frequency, approaching the Frank-Sabin lower bound with power-law gap ∼α⁻⁰·⁹.
AI.conclusion: The pipeline validates known cases and serves as a discovery tool. The authors conjecture the Airy-Strichartz supremum equals the Frank-Sabin bound, approached but not attained along the breather family.

title: Error analysis for learning fractional stochastic differential equations with applications in neural approximations
summary: This paper develops a framework for the error analysis in nonparametric model fitting of fractional stochastic differential equations based on discrete observations. We identify and quantify the main error sources -- time discretization, coefficient approximation, and model fitting error -- within a unified framework. Through Sobolev-type norms, we derive convergence rates that incorporate the regularity of trajectories, thereby capturing the interaction of these error components. To demonstrate the applicability of the theory, we introduce a training scheme for coefficient function estimation based on shallow neural networks and a recurrent architecture. Numerical experiments validate the theoretical findings and illustrate the effectiveness of the approach.

AI.tldr: A unified error analysis framework for nonparametric model fitting of fractional SDEs, with convergence rates tied to trajectory regularity, validated via neural network-based training.
AI.motivation: To identify and quantify the main error sources (time discretization, coefficient approximation, model fitting) in nonparametric fitting of fractional SDEs under a unified analytical framework.
AI.method: Uses Sobolev-type norms to derive convergence rates incorporating trajectory regularity; employs shallow neural networks with recurrent architecture for coefficient function estimation.
AI.result: Convergence rates that capture the interaction of discretization, approximation, and fitting errors are derived; numerical experiments confirm theoretical findings.
AI.conclusion: The framework effectively quantifies and separates error sources in fractional SDE fitting, and the proposed neural network scheme is practically effective.

title: Lifespan of Classical Solutions to One-Dimensional Quasilinear Wave Equations
summary: In this paper, we consider the upper and lower bounds of the lifespan of classical solutions of the Cauchy problem for the one-dimensional quasilinear wave equation $u_{tt}-c(u_x)^2u_{xx}=0$ where the derivative of $c(θ)$ tends to $0$ near the origin. In particular, our result shows that the lifespan of the solution extends algebraically depending on the smallness of the initial data. Furthermore, we also show that when $c(θ)$ is flat at the origin, the lifespan extends exponentially depending on the smallness of the initial data. Our proof is based on the method of Lax's characteristics and Riemann invariants.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes the lifespan of classical solutions for a one-dimensional quasilinear wave equation, showing algebraic or exponential extension depending on how flat the wave speed function is at the origin, using Lax's characteristics and Riemann invariants.
AI.motivation: To understand how the lifespan of classical solutions to quasilinear wave equations depends on the smallness of initial data, particularly when the wave speed derivative vanishes near the origin.
AI.method: The proof employs Lax's method of characteristics and Riemann invariants to analyze the behavior of solutions to the Cauchy problem for $u_{tt}-c(u_x)^2u_{xx}=0$.
AI.result: The lifespan extends algebraically with small initial data in general, and extends exponentially when $c(\theta)$ is flat at the origin.
AI.conclusion: The flatness of the wave speed function at the origin significantly enhances the lifespan of classical solutions, from algebraic to exponential dependence on the smallness of initial data.

title: Calculating Domain of Attraction Boundary of Power Systems Based on the Gentlest Ascent Dynamics
summary: The power system, a fundamental public utility, is increasingly important due to growing global electricity demand. Recent large-scale blackouts (e.g., Iberian Peninsula, UK) have raised concerns about transient stability under impact faults. Transient stability is determined by post-disturbance synchronizing capability of synchronous generators, formulated as identifying the domain of attraction (DOA) boundary of the asymptotically stable equilibrium. Using a benchmark model of synchronous-generator-dominated power systems, this report employs a gentlest ascent dynamics (GAD) method for 1-saddle points, an adjoint operator method for periodic orbits, and stable manifold algorithms to compute the DOA boundary. These algorithms transform DOA boundary determination into constructing unstable critical elements (saddle points and periodic orbits) and their stable manifolds. Theoretically, under certain assumptions we prove that the DOA boundary is the closure of the union of stable manifolds of index-1 critical elements, and establish a stability theory for a perturbed GAD system. Numerical experiments on two-machine and three-machine systems (with only saddle points or with periodic orbits) validate the effectiveness and accuracy. Results show the algorithms accurately capture the geometric structure of the DOA boundary, providing a new numerical tool for transient stability analysis.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes using gentlest ascent dynamics and stable manifold algorithms to compute the domain of attraction boundary for transient stability analysis in power systems, validated on two-machine and three-machine systems.
AI.motivation: Increasing global electricity demand and recent large-scale blackouts have raised concerns about transient stability under impact faults, motivating the need for better methods to determine the domain of attraction boundary.
AI.method: The authors employ gentlest ascent dynamics (GAD) for 1-saddle points, an adjoint operator method for periodic orbits, and stable manifold algorithms to compute the DOA boundary by constructing unstable critical elements and their stable manifolds.
AI.result: Numerical experiments on two-machine and three-machine systems validate the effectiveness and accuracy, showing the algorithms accurately capture the geometric structure of the DOA boundary.
AI.conclusion: The proposed methods provide a new numerical tool for transient stability analysis, with theoretical proof that the DOA boundary is the closure of the union of stable manifolds of index-1 critical elements.

title: Reconstruction of non-self-adjoint anisotropic and complex inclusions in the Calderón problem
summary: We generalize recent results on the monotonicity method, for inclusion detection in the partial data anisotropic Calderón problem, to very general non-self-adjoint perturbations. This involves a forward model that accounts for both the anisotropic real conductivity and the anisotropic permittivity, and the results hold in any spatial dimension $d \geq 2$. We assume that the inclusion boundaries can be reached from the domain boundary via a set on which the background conductivity is self-adjoint, and that a definiteness condition holds near the inclusion boundaries. Away from the inclusion boundaries we allow general $L^\infty$ non-self-adjoint perturbations. We only require unique continuation based on the self-adjoint part of the background conductivity, thus making the methods compatible with generic unique continuation results.

AI.tldr: Generalizes the monotonicity method for inclusion detection in the anisotropic Calderón problem with partial data to handle non-self-adjoint perturbations, incorporating both anisotropic real conductivity and permittivity in any dimension d≥2.
AI.motivation: To extend the monotonicity method for detecting inclusions in anisotropic media to more realistic physical scenarios involving non-self-adjoint (lossy/dissipative) perturbations, which are common in electromagnetics and other wave phenomena.
AI.method: Assumes inclusion boundaries are reachable from the domain boundary via a self-adjoint background region, imposes a definiteness condition near inclusion boundaries, and allows general L∞ non-self-adjoint perturbations elsewhere. Uses unique continuation based only on the self-adjoint part of the background conductivity.
AI.result: Successfully generalizes the monotonicity method to handle very general non-self-adjoint perturbations in the anisotropic Calderón problem with partial data, for any spatial dimension d≥2.
AI.conclusion: The monotonicity method is robust to non-self-adjoint perturbations under mild conditions (reachable boundaries, definiteness near boundaries), making it compatible with generic unique continuation results and applicable to more realistic anisotropic media.

title: Probabilistic Floating-Point Round-Off Analysis via Concentration Inequalities
comment: Long version of the eponymous OOPSLA 2026 paper
summary:
Floating-point round-off errors are ubiquitous in numerically intensive programs arising in fields such as scientific computing and optimization. As floating-point errors potentially lead to unexpected and catastrophic program failures, one must derive guaranteed round-off thresholds to ensure the correctness of these programs. However, deterministic round-off thresholds tend to be too conservative to be usable in practice, since they often involve large round-off errors that occur with small probability. Probabilistic thresholds relax deterministic ones by specifying that the probability of the round-off error exceeding a threshold is below a given confidence.
  In this work, we propose a novel approach to probabilistic round-off analysis, by applying concentration inequalities over the Taylor expansion from FPTaylor (TOPLAS 2018). A major obstacle in applying concentration inequalities is that the Taylor expansion involves absolute value operators that make the calculation of the expected values of the first order partial differential terms difficult. Our first step to overcome this obstacle is a sound over-approximation that removes the absolute value operators in polynomial expressions. Then, we show how to handle fractional expressions by a transformation into polynomial case. Finally, we show how to improve our approach with range partitioning. Our approach is scalable since the key computational part is the calculation of expected values of polynomial expressions with independent variables, for which the linear and independence properties of expectation boost the computation. Experimental results show that our approach is orders of magnitude more time efficient, while producing thresholds with comparable precision against the state of the art.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a novel approach to probabilistic round-off error analysis that applies concentration inequalities over Taylor expansions, overcoming key obstacles (absolute value operators, fractional expressions) using sound over-approximations and range partitioning, achieving orders-of-magnitude speedup with comparable precision to state-of-the-art methods.
AI.motivation: Floating-point round-off errors can cause catastrophic program failures in scientific computing and optimization. Deterministic round-off thresholds are often too conservative for practical use because they account for large errors that occur with low probability. Probabilistic thresholds offer a more practical alternative by bounding the probability that errors exceed a given threshold.
AI.method: The authors apply concentration inequalities over Taylor expansions (from FPTaylor). They overcome three key challenges: (1) removing absolute value operators in polynomial expressions via sound over-approximation, (2) handling fractional expressions by transforming them into polynomial form, and (3) improving precision through range partitioning. The computation leverages linearity and independence properties of expectation for efficiency.
AI.result: Experimental results show the approach is orders of magnitude more time efficient than state-of-the-art methods, while producing probabilistic round-off thresholds with comparable precision.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method demonstrates that probabilistic round-off analysis can be both scalable and precise, offering a practical alternative to overly conservative deterministic thresholds for numerically intensive programs.

title: Ill-posedness in the critical Sobolev space for the $b$-Novikov equation
summary: This article proves norm inflation in the critical Sobolev space $H^{3/2}(\mathbb{R})$ for the $b$-Novikov equation, which is a $1$-parameter family of Camassa-Holm-type equations with cubic nonlinearities. This result completes the well-posedness theory for this equation, which was previously known to be locally well-posed in $H^{s}(\mathbb{R})$ for $s>3/2$ and ill-posed in $H^{s}(\mathbb{R})$ for $s<3/2$.

AI.tldr: This paper proves norm inflation in the critical Sobolev space H^{3/2}(ℝ) for the b-Novikov equation, completing the well-posedness theory by showing the critical threshold s=3/2 is where the equation transitions from well-posed to ill-posed.
AI.motivation: To complete the well-posedness theory for the b-Novikov equation by determining whether the critical Sobolev space H^{3/2}(ℝ) is well-posed or ill-posed, as previous results only covered s>3/2 (well-posed) and s<3/2 (ill-posed).
AI.method: The authors prove norm inflation in H^{3/2}(ℝ), meaning that for any ε>0, there exist smooth initial data arbitrarily small in the H^{3/2} norm such that the solution becomes arbitrarily large in the H^{3/2} norm within arbitrarily small time.
AI.result: Norm inflation holds in the critical space H^{3/2}(ℝ) for the b-Novikov equation, establishing that s=3/2 is a sharp threshold where the equation is ill-posed.
AI.conclusion: The well-posedness theory for the b-Novikov equation is now complete: locally well-posed for s>3/2, ill-posed (norm inflation) at s=3/2, and ill-posed for s<3/2.

title: From Video-to-PDE: Data-Driven Discovery of Nonlinear Dye Plume Dynamics
summary: Inferring continuum models directly from video is hampered by two facts: the recorded field is uncalibrated image intensity rather than a physical state, and direct numerical differentiation of noisy frames is unstable. We develop a video-to-PDE pipeline that converts grayscale recordings of an ink plume into a normalised scalar field $u(x,y,t)$, isolates a bulk drift $\mathbf{v}(t)$ from intrinsic spreading via the intensity-weighted centroid, and identifies an effective transport law by weak-form sparse regression. Conditioning, threshold-sweep and random-centre diagnostics show that overcomplete libraries are strongly collinear; the search is therefore restricted to compact gradient-based libraries. Coefficients are refined by an inverse physics-informed network and recalibrated against forward rollouts, with a chronological block bootstrap quantifying uncertainty. The selected reduced model $u_t+\mathbf v(t)\!\cdot\!\nabla u = 9.005\,|\nabla u|^{2}+0.666\,Δu$ outperforms advection--diffusion baselines on held-out frames, retains a positive Laplacian coefficient, and admits a Cole--Hopf reduction to a linear advection--diffusion equation. The framework demonstrates that uncalibrated visual data can yield compact, predictive and structurally interpretable continuum models when discovery, calibration and uncertainty are treated as distinct stages.

AI.tldr: The paper develops a video-to-PDE pipeline that extracts interpretable continuum models from uncalibrated grayscale video recordings of an ink plume, yielding a compact PDE (advection with nonlinear diffusion) validated through inverse physics-informed networks and uncertainty quantification.
AI.motivation: Inferring continuum models from video is hampered by uncalibrated image intensity (not a physical state) and unstable numerical differentiation of noisy frames, motivating a structured pipeline that treats discovery, calibration, and uncertainty as distinct stages.
AI.method: Grayscale video → normalized scalar field u(x,y,t); isolate bulk drift v(t) via intensity-weighted centroid; identify transport law via weak-form sparse regression (restricted to compact gradient-based libraries to avoid collinearity); refine coefficients with inverse physics-informed network; recalibrate against forward rollouts; quantify uncertainty via chronological block bootstrap.
AI.result: Selected reduced model: u_t + v(t)·∇u = 9.005|∇u|² + 0.666Δu, outperforming advection-diffusion baselines on held-out frames, retaining a positive Laplacian coefficient, and admitting a Cole-Hopf reduction to a linear advection-diffusion equation.
AI.conclusion: Uncalibrated visual data can yield compact, predictive, and structurally interpretable continuum models when discovery, calibration, and uncertainty are treated as distinct stages.

title: On a Partial Voigt Regularization of the 3D Magnetohydrodynamic Equations in Velocity-Vorticity Form
summary: The Velocity-Vorticity (VV) formulation of the incompressible Navier-Stokes equations has become popular in recent years, especially in numerical studies, due to its structural advantages. Recently, with L. Rebholz, we introduced a Voigt regularization to the momentum equation in this formulation, establishing global well-posedness of the regularized system in 3D, along with convergence results and a blow-up criterion. In the present work, we extend these ideas to the 3D magnetohydrodynamics (MHD) equations. While it may seem that a ``VV-type'' split on the magnetic equation is required, we show that no such modification is necessary, and global well-posedness holds with a Voigt regularization only on the momentum equation, preserving the structure of both the vorticity and magnetic equations. We also prove that the regularized system converges to the original system, up to a possible blow-up time, and we establish a blow-up criterion for solutions to the original 3D MHD system.

AI.tldr: This paper extends a Voigt-regularized Velocity-Vorticity formulation to 3D MHD equations, proving global well-posedness with regularization only on the momentum equation, convergence results, and a blow-up criterion.
AI.motivation: To extend the previously developed Voigt-regularized VV formulation (designed for Navier-Stokes) to the more complex 3D MHD equations, aiming to achieve global well-posedness while preserving the natural structure of the vorticity and magnetic equations.
AI.method: The authors apply a Voigt regularization only to the momentum equation (not the magnetic equation) in the VV formulation of the 3D MHD system. They analyze global well-posedness, convergence to the original system, and establish a blow-up criterion.
AI.result: Global well-posedness of the regularized 3D MHD system is achieved with Voigt regularization applied solely to the momentum equation, without needing modification of the magnetic equation. The regularized solutions converge to the original system up to a possible blow-up time.
AI.conclusion: The VV-type Voigt regularization approach successfully extends to 3D MHD equations, preserving desirable structural properties while ensuring global well-posedness and providing a blow-up criterion for the original system.

title: Sequential topology optimization: SIMP initialization for level-set boundary refinement
comment: 19 pages, 7 figures, 5 tables. Submitted to Advances in Engineering Software. Source code: https://github.com/jezekon/2026-Jezek-SeqTopOpt. Archived snapshot with reproduction data: https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.20024424
summary: Density-based topology optimization methods such as SIMP enable efficient topological exploration but produce diffuse material boundaries that require interpretation before manufacturing. Level-set methods maintain sharp interfaces but are sensitive to the initial design. This paper presents a sequential framework that addresses these complementary limitations through a signed distance function (SDF)-based geometry transfer, formulated for three-dimensional meshes. The SIMP density distribution is converted into an SDF that initializes subsequent level-set boundary refinement. From the level-set perspective, the SIMP-derived initialization mitigates sensitivity to the initial design. From the SIMP perspective, the level-set stage acts as optimization-driven post-processing that produces manufacturing-ready boundaries. Validation on three-dimensional cantilever and MBB benchmarks demonstrates compliance comparable to standalone level-set optimization, with up to 4.6x wall-clock speedup on the cantilever case. The full implementation is released under an open-source license to support reproducibility.

AI.tldr: A sequential framework combining SIMP topology optimization with level-set refinement via signed distance function transfer, achieving sharp, manufacturing-ready boundaries with up to 4.6x speedup on 3D benchmarks.
AI.motivation: SIMP produces diffuse boundaries unsuitable for direct manufacturing; level-set methods are sensitive to initial designs. The paper aims to combine both methods' strengths while mitigating their weaknesses.
AI.method: Converts SIMP density distribution into a signed distance function (SDF) on 3D meshes, which initializes level-set boundary refinement — creating a sequential SIMP→level-set pipeline.
AI.result: Compliance comparable to standalone level-set optimization, with up to 4.6x wall-clock speedup on 3D cantilever benchmark; open-source implementation released.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework effectively bridges SIMP and level-set methods, delivering manufacturing-ready boundaries with improved computational efficiency.

title: Numerical study of the 2D Kaup-Broer-Kuperschmidt Boussinesq system
summary: In this work we consider the well posed version of the Kaup-Broer-Kuperschmidt system in two dimensions. We numerically construct soliton type solutions and show that they are unstable both against dispersion and singularity formation. Further, we study line solitons and their stability, as well as generally localised initial data. In either case we fail to find stable structures.

AI.tldr: The paper studies the 2D Kaup-Broer-Kuperschmidt system, finding that soliton solutions are unstable against dispersion and singularity formation, with no stable structures observed.
AI.motivation: To investigate the well-posed version of the Kaup-Broer-Kuperschmidt system in two dimensions and examine the existence and stability of soliton-type solutions.
AI.method: Numerical construction of soliton solutions and stability analysis through studying line solitons, localized initial data, and their evolution.
AI.result: Soliton solutions are unstable against both dispersion and singularity formation; no stable structures found for line solitons or localized initial data.
AI.conclusion: The 2D Kaup-Broer-Kuperschmidt system does not support stable soliton structures, exhibiting only unstable behavior.

title: Sharp Dirichlet eigenvalue inequalities on triangles
summary: We prove sharp Dirichlet eigenvalue inequalities for planar triangles. We settle a conjecture of Laugesen and Siudeja by showing that the equilateral triangle uniquely minimizes a scale-invariant functional of the first Dirichlet eigenvalue, area, and perimeter. Consequences include an optimal two-term lower bound for the first Dirichlet eigenvalue in terms of area and perimeter. We also prove a Cheeger-type inequality with an explicit best constant considered by Parini. To prove these conjectures we propose a new method for proving Dirichlet eigenvalue inequalities on triangles. Our method is based on a new computable lower bound for second-order directional shape derivatives under vertex perturbations. It also uses validated finite-element error estimates and recently developed analytic estimates for eigenvalues of nearly degenerate triangles. The method is not specific to the functionals considered in this paper and it can be used to prove various other eigenvalue inequalities on triangles.

AI.tldr: The paper proves sharp Dirichlet eigenvalue inequalities for planar triangles, settling a conjecture by Laugesen and Siudeja that the equilateral triangle uniquely minimizes a scale-invariant functional of the first Dirichlet eigenvalue, area, and perimeter. The authors develop a new method using directional shape derivatives, validated finite-element error estimates, and analytic estimates for nearly degenerate triangles.
AI.motivation: To settle open conjectures about optimal Dirichlet eigenvalue inequalities on triangles, specifically the conjecture by Laugesen and Siudeja regarding the equilateral triangle's minimizing property, and to prove a Cheeger-type inequality with explicit best constant.
AI.method: A new method combining (1) computable lower bounds for second-order directional shape derivatives under vertex perturbations, (2) validated finite-element error estimates, and (3) recently developed analytic estimates for eigenvalues of nearly degenerate triangles.
AI.result: Proved that the equilateral triangle uniquely minimizes a scale-invariant functional of the first Dirichlet eigenvalue, area, and perimeter. Established an optimal two-term lower bound for the first Dirichlet eigenvalue. Proved a Cheeger-type inequality with explicit best constant.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method is not limited to the functionals considered and can be applied to prove various other eigenvalue inequalities on triangles.

title: Understanding In-Context Learning for Nonlinear Regression with Transformers: Attention as Featurizer
summary: Pre-trained transformers are able to learn from examples provided as part of the prompt without any weight updates, a remarkable ability known as in-context learning (ICL). Despite its demonstrated efficacy across various domains, the theoretical understanding of ICL is still developing. Whereas most existing theory has focused on linear models, we study ICL in the nonlinear regression setting. Through the interaction mechanism in attention, we explicitly construct transformer networks to realize nonlinear features, such as polynomial or spline bases, which span a wide class of functions. Based on this construction, we establish a framework to analyze end-to-end in-context nonlinear regression with the constructed features. Our theory provides finite-sample generalization error bounds in terms of context length and training set size. We numerically validate the theory on synthetic regression tasks.

AI.tldr: This paper theoretically analyzes in-context learning for nonlinear regression using transformers, constructing explicit attention-based networks that realize polynomial and spline features, and providing finite-sample generalization bounds.
AI.motivation: Existing ICL theory focuses mostly on linear models; this work extends theoretical understanding to the more practical nonlinear regression setting.
AI.method: The authors explicitly construct transformer networks using attention mechanisms to realize nonlinear features (polynomial and spline bases), then develop a theoretical framework for end-to-end in-context nonlinear regression analysis.
AI.result: Provides finite-sample generalization error bounds derived in terms of context length and training set size, with numerical validation on synthetic regression tasks.
AI.conclusion: The construction and analysis demonstrate that transformers can theoretically perform ICL for nonlinear regression with provable guarantees, extending ICL theory beyond linear models.

title: Topology of gradient Ricci shrinkers via weighted $L^2$ cohomology
summary: This paper proves several topological results for smooth gradient Ricci shrinkers. We establish upper bounds for the Betti numbers, a vanishing theorem for cohomology, and a dichotomy for the number of ends. We also prove a full Hodge theorem for a large class of shrinkers. The methods are based on weighted $L^2$ cohomology and extend to self-shrinkers of the mean curvature flow.

AI.tldr: This paper proves topological results for smooth gradient Ricci shrinkers, including Betti number bounds, cohomology vanishing, end dichotomy, and a Hodge theorem using weighted L² cohomology methods that extend to mean curvature flow self-shrinkers.
AI.motivation: To establish topological constraints and classification results for gradient Ricci shrinkers, which are important singularity models in Ricci flow.
AI.method: Weighted L² cohomology theory applied to gradient Ricci shrinkers, with extensions to self-shrinkers of mean curvature flow.
AI.result: Upper bounds for Betti numbers, a vanishing theorem for cohomology, a dichotomy for the number of ends, and a full Hodge theorem for a large class of shrinkers.
AI.conclusion: These topological results advance understanding of the structure of Ricci shrinkers and their classification, with applications also to mean curvature flow.

title: Riesz property in the case of multiple eigenvalues
summary: We analyze spectra and the Riesz property of spectral projections of non-symmetric perturbations of self-adjoint operators with eigenvalues having arbitrary multiplicities, including infinite ones. In particular, we establish the Riesz property for perturbations of the multi-dimensional harmonic oscillator, Landau Hamiltonian and Laplace-Beltrami operator on a sphere by complex-valued $L^r$-potentials if $d/2 < r < \infty$.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes spectra and the Riesz property of spectral projections for non-symmetric perturbations of self-adjoint operators with eigenvalues of arbitrary (including infinite) multiplicities. It establishes the Riesz property for perturbations of multi-dimensional harmonic oscillators, Landau Hamiltonians, and Laplace-Beltrami operators on spheres by complex-valued L^r-potentials when d/2 < r < ∞.
AI.motivation: To understand the spectral behavior and Riesz property of spectral projections when self-adjoint operators are perturbed non-symmetrically, especially for operators with eigenvalues that may have infinite multiplicities—a more general case than previously studied.
AI.method: The authors analyze spectra and the Riesz property of spectral projections under non-symmetric perturbations, extending the theory to cover arbitrary multiplicities (including infinite). Concrete applications include perturbations of multi-dimensional harmonic oscillators, Landau Hamiltonians, and Laplace-Beltrami operators by complex-valued L^r-potentials.
AI.result: The Riesz property is established for perturbations of key operators (harmonic oscillator, Landau Hamiltonian, Laplace-Beltrami operator on a sphere) by complex-valued L^r-potentials under the condition d/2 < r < ∞.
AI.conclusion: The main results extend the understanding of spectral projections' Riesz property to a broader class of operators with arbitrary eigenvalue multiplicities, including infinite-dimensional eigenspaces, under non-symmetric perturbations.

title: Projected Evolutionary Lifting and Well-Posedness of Stationary Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman Equations in Infinite Dimensions
summary: This paper establishes the existence and uniqueness of mild solutions to stationary Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman (HJB) equations associated with infinite-horizon stochastic optimal control problems in separable Hilbert spaces. Our framework includes settings with a lack of global smoothing properties of the transition semigroup, singular dynamics involving unbounded control operators, and state-dependent running costs. We overcome these challenges by lifting the state space using the Projected Evolutionary Lifting technique. This work is an extension of G. Bolli and F. Gozzi, Lifting and partial smoothing for stationary HJB equations and related control problems in infinite dimensions, 2025, in which existence and uniqueness is proved via a contraction mapping argument and is consequently restricted to sufficiently large discount factors. We remove this restriction, proving existence and uniqueness for any discount rate $λ> 0$ using tools from the theory of maximally monotone operators.

AI.tldr: This paper proves existence and uniqueness of mild solutions to stationary HJB equations in infinite-dimensional spaces for any discount factor λ>0, extending prior work that was restricted to large discount factors. It uses the Projected Evolutionary Lifting technique and maximally monotone operator theory to overcome challenges like lack of global smoothing and singular control dynamics.
AI.motivation: To establish existence and uniqueness of solutions to stationary HJB equations in infinite-dimensional settings where standard smoothing properties fail, unbounded control operators appear, and state-dependent costs are present—without the restriction to large discount factors that limited previous work.
AI.method: The paper lifts the state space using the Projected Evolutionary Lifting technique and applies tools from the theory of maximally monotone operators to remove the discount factor restriction, proving existence and uniqueness for any λ > 0.
AI.result: Existence and uniqueness of mild solutions are established for any discount rate λ > 0, eliminating the earlier restriction to sufficiently large discount factors imposed by contraction mapping arguments.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework successfully handles infinite-dimensional stationary HJB equations with lack of global smoothing, unbounded control operators, and state-dependent costs, with solutions guaranteed for any positive discount factor.

title: An Optimal Regularity Theory for Immersed Stable Minimal Hypersurfaces with Small Singular Set
summary: We show that if $M^n$ is a properly immersed, two-sided, stable minimal hypersurface in $B^{n+1}_1(0)\setminus S$, where $S$ is closed with $\mathcal{H}^{n-2}(S)=0$, then $\text{dim}_{\mathcal{H}}\text{sing}(M)\leq n-7$, namely $\overline{M}\cap B^{n+1}_1(0)$ is represented by a smooth minimal immersion outside a closed set of generally unavoidable singularities which has Hausdorff dimension at most $n-7$. This provides the optimal a priori size assumption on the non-immersed singular set in order to guarantee optimal regularity. Consequently, such objects form a compact class under mass upper bounds.

AI.tldr: The paper proves that a two-sided stable minimal hypersurface in a ball missing a small singular set (of codimension at least 2) has regularity with singular set of Hausdorff dimension at most n-7, which is optimal.
AI.motivation: To determine the minimal a priori size assumption on the singular set that guarantees optimal regularity (dimension ≤ n-7) for stable minimal hypersurfaces.
AI.method: Uses techniques from geometric measure theory and regularity theory for minimal surfaces, analyzing stable minimal hypersurfaces in balls with punctured singular sets.
AI.result: If the singular set S has ℋ^{n-2}(S)=0, then the Hausdorff dimension of the singular set of M is at most n-7, which is optimal. Moreover, such objects form a compact class under mass bounds.
AI.conclusion: This establishes the optimal regularity result for stable minimal hypersurfaces with controlled singular sets, confirming that the n-7 dimension bound is unavoidable.

title: Grokability in five inequalities
summary: In this note, we report five mathematical discoveries made in collaboration with Grok, all of which have been subsequently verified by the authors. These include an improved lower bound on the maximal Gaussian perimeter of convex sets in $\mathbb{R}^n$, sharper $L_2$-$L_1$ moment comparison inequalities on the Hamming cube $\{-1,1\}^n$, a strengthened autoconvolution inequality, improved asymptotic bounds on the size of the largest $g$-Sidon sets in $\{1,\dots,n\}$, and an optimal balanced Szarek's inequality.

AI.tldr: This paper reports five mathematical discoveries made collaboratively with Grok, verified by the authors, covering inequalities in convex geometry, probability on the Hamming cube, autoconvolution, Sidon sets, and Szarek's inequality.
AI.motivation: To present and verify new mathematical results discovered through human-AI collaboration, demonstrating the potential of AI-assisted mathematical research.
AI.method: Collaborative mathematical reasoning between human authors and Grok, followed by formal verification of the discovered results.
AI.result: Five new results: improved lower bound on Gaussian perimeter of convex sets, sharper L2-L1 moment comparison inequalities on the Hamming cube, a strengthened autoconvolution inequality, improved asymptotic bounds on largest g-Sidon sets, and an optimal balanced Szarek's inequality.
AI.conclusion: The verified discoveries show that AI collaboration can lead to meaningful mathematical contributions.
